content.content: Matter is something which has mass, occupies space and can be perceived by our senses. e.g. Hydrogen, milk, oxygen, pen, table, water, iron, air, oil, sugar etc.
content.explanation: Matter is anything that you can touch, taste, or smell in the physical world. Since it has mass, it means it weighs something and takes up a certain amount of room.
content.teacherTip: Use the acronym "MOS" - Mass, Occupies space, Senses - to remember what matter is.
content.examTip: When giving examples of matter, try to include one solid, one liquid, and one gas to show complete understanding.

content.content: Matter is composed of tiny particles called molecules, which are in constant motion, have spaces between them and have inter-molecular attraction.
content.explanation: Even though matter looks solid or continuous, it is actually made of billions of tiny pieces. These pieces never stay still and are always pulling on each other.
content.teacherTip: Imagine molecules like a crowd of people constantly moving and jostling in a room.
content.examTip: Always mention "constant motion" when describing the molecular nature of matter.

content.content: Every molecule can exist freely in nature and has all the properties of matter.
content.explanation: A single molecule of water still behaves exactly like water and has the same chemical traits. It is the smallest part of a substance that can stay on its own.
content.teacherTip: Think of a molecule as the smallest "representative" of a substance.
content.examTip: Use the word "freely" specifically when defining a molecule.

content.content: A molecule is composed of ATOMS, but atom cannot exist free in nature.
content.explanation: Atoms are like the individual LEGO bricks that snap together to build a molecule. While the molecule is stable alone, the individual atoms are usually too reactive to stay separate.
content.teacherTip: Atoms are the "building blocks," and molecules are the "buildings."
content.examTip: Remember that the main difference between an atom and a molecule in this context is the ability to exist independently.

content.content: The molecules of a matter exert a force of attraction on each other. The force of attraction is called INTER-MOLECULAR FORCE. This force in solid is very strong. In liquids this force is less strong and in molecules of gas it is very less.
content.explanation: This is the invisible "glue" that holds matter together and determines if it is a solid, liquid, or gas. The stronger this force, the closer the molecules stay to one another.
content.teacherTip: Think of "Inter" as "between," so this is the force "between molecules." Solid = Strong, Liquid = Loose, Gas = Ghostly weak.
content.examTip: Don't confuse this with intra-molecular forces; focus on the attraction between separate molecules.

content.content: The inter-molecular force of attraction between the molecules of SAME substance is called FORCE OF COHESION. e.g. between water and water.
content.explanation: Cohesion is what allows water to form droplets because the water molecules want to stick to their own kind.
content.teacherTip: "Co" means together with its own kind - like "co-workers" of the same company.
content.examTip: Mention "same substance" to get full marks for the definition of cohesion.

content.content: The force of attraction between the molecules of two DIFFERENT substances is called FORCE OF ADHESION. e.g. between glue and paper.
content.explanation: Adhesion is why water sticks to a glass or why paint sticks to a wall. It involves the attraction between two completely different types of molecules.
content.teacherTip: Think of "Adhesive tape" which is designed to stick to different things.
content.examTip: Use the example of "glue and paper" to illustrate adhesion clearly in your answer.

content.content: Take 50 c.c. of water in a measuring cylinder. Add a small quantity of salt in it. Salt gets dissolved in water and still level remains at 50 c.c. The salt molecules enter into spaces of water and water molecules into spaces of salt molecules.
content.explanation: This experiment proves that matter is not "solid" all the way through but has tiny gaps. Because the salt fits into the gaps of the water, the total volume doesn't increase.
content.teacherTip: Think of this like pouring sand into a jar full of large marbles; the sand fills the gaps.
content.examTip: When describing this experiment, emphasize that the water level "remains the same."

content.content: The molecules of matter are in constant motion. Opening a bottle of perfume in a corner of room - its smell reaches the other parts of the room. This natural spreading is called diffusion.
content.explanation: Perfume molecules move through the air by bumping into air molecules and spreading out.
content.teacherTip: Smelling food from the kitchen in your bedroom is the perfect real-life example of molecular motion.
content.examTip: This example is often used to prove that molecules in gases move very fast and freely.

content.content: Change of solid directly into vapours on absorbing heat is called SUBLIMATION. Examples: camphor, naphthalene balls, dry ice.
content.explanation: Some substances skip the liquid phase entirely when they get hot enough.
content.teacherTip: Sublimation is like taking a "shortcut" from solid to gas.
content.examTip: Always mention that it happens "directly" without turning into a liquid first.

content.content: The change of vapours directly into solid on cooling is called DEPOSITION. Also known as desublimation.
content.explanation: Deposition is the exact opposite of sublimation, where a gas turns into a solid instantly when it hits a cold surface. This is how frost forms on windows during a very cold night.
content.teacherTip: Think of "depositing" or "leaving behind" a solid layer from the air.
content.examTip: Deposition is also known as "desublimation" — know both terms.

content.content: MELTING: Change of solid to liquid at fixed temperature on heating. FUSION/FREEZING: Change of liquid to solid state on cooling at a fixed temperature. Freezing is the reverse of melting.
content.explanation: When you heat a solid, the molecules start vibrating so fast that they break free. Freezing involves removing heat so molecules slow down and get trapped again.
content.teacherTip: For water, the melting point and freezing point are the same (0°C).
content.examTip: Use the term "fixed temperature" to describe the melting point accurately.

content.content: EVAPORATION: Change of liquid to vapours from the surface of liquid. It is a surface phenomenon. Takes place at ALL temperatures. Has a cooling effect.
content.explanation: Molecules at the top of a liquid escape into the air, even if the liquid isn't boiling. Because the hottest molecules escape first, the liquid left behind feels colder.
content.teacherTip: Sweating is our body's way of using the "cooling effect" of evaporation.
content.examTip: Distinguish evaporation from boiling by noting it happens at "all temperatures."

content.content: VAPORIZATION: Change of liquid to vapour state on heating at constant temperature. BOILING POINT: The temperature at which a liquid starts changing into vapours at constant temperature. Boiling point of water is 100°C.
content.explanation: Vaporization (or boiling) happens throughout the entire liquid once it reaches a specific temperature. It is rapid compared to evaporation.
content.teacherTip: Think of boiling water in a kettle as "forced" vaporization using a stove.
content.examTip: Specify that vaporization occurs at a "constant temperature" called the boiling point.

content.content: CONDENSATION: The change of vapours on cooling at fixed temperature to liquid. Example: Steam at 100°C condenses to water at 100°C on cooling.
content.explanation: Condensation is what creates the "sweat" on a cold soda can as water vapour in the air cools down.
content.teacherTip: "Condensation" is "gas to liquid."
content.examTip: Mention "cooling" for condensation and "heating" for sublimation.

content.content: SOLID → (Melting/Heat absorbed) → LIQUID → (Vaporization/Heat absorbed) → GAS. GAS → (Condensation/Heat rejected) → LIQUID → (Freezing/Heat rejected) → SOLID. SOLID → (Sublimation) → GAS. GAS → (Deposition) → SOLID.
content.explanation: Matter can change its physical form just by gaining or losing heat energy. The temperature stays the same during actual changes because the energy is busy rearranging molecules.
content.teacherTip: Absorption of heat makes things "looser" (solid to gas), while rejection makes them "tighter" (gas to solid).
content.examTip: Practice drawing the cycle with arrows to visualize how heat moving in or out drives the change.

content.content: Three factors affecting rate of evaporation: (i) Area of exposed surface (ii) Temperature of liquid (iii) Nature of the liquid (iv) Presence of humidity.
content.explanation: Evaporation goes faster if you spread the liquid out, heat it up, or if the air around it is dry. Some liquids like alcohol evaporate much faster than water.
content.teacherTip: Think of "Hot, Dry, and Wide" for fast evaporation.
content.examTip: Mentioning "Area of exposed surface" is a very common point in scoring full marks.

content.question: The temperature of a substance remains unaffected during its change of state.
content.answer: True. All the heat energy being added is used to break bonds between molecules rather than raising the temperature.
content.teacherTip: During a phase change, the thermometer "takes a break" and stays still.To make this simple, think of a substance as a community of tiny particles. When you heat a substance, the energy you supply can only do one of two things at any given time: 	1.	Make the particles move faster (which raises the temperature). 	2.	Pull the particles further apart by breaking their bonds (which changes the state). During a phase change (like ice melting into water), the particles are busy breaking bonds, so they cannot speed up. The Simple Model: The "Spring" Analogy Imagine two particles connected by a strong metal spring. •	To raise temperature: You make the particles vibrate back and forth rapidly. The faster they shake, the higher the thermometer goes. •	To change state: You have to pull the particles so far apart that the spring permanently snaps or stretches out. When you apply heat during a change of state, all the energy goes into stretching and snapping that spring. Because the energy is trapped in the stretched spring, the particles don't actually shake any faster than they did before. Since they aren't shaking faster, the temperature stays exactly the same. The Simple Formula Usually, when you heat something, you use the formula: ‭$$\text{Heat} = \text{Mass} \times \text{Specific Heat} \times \text{Change in Temperature}$$‬‭‬‭‬‭‬ But during a phase change, the "Change in Temperature" is zero. To calculate the energy required to break those bonds, we switch to a much simpler formula: ‭$$Q = m \cdot L$$‬‭‬‭‬ Where: •	‭$Q$‬ = The total heat energy added (or removed). •	‭$m$‬ = The mass of the substance. •	‭$L$‬ = The Latent Heat (a fixed number that represents the hidden energy needed to snap the "springs" for 1 kg of that specific substance). Because the temperature change (‭$\Delta T$‬‭‬) is not part of this equation, the thermometer stays completely still until every single bond is broken.
content.examTip: This "hidden heat" is called Latent Heat.

content.question: Ice melts at 100°C.
content.answer: False. Ice melts at 0°C by absorption of heat. 100°C is where liquid water turns into steam.
content.teacherTip: 0 is for ice, 100 is for steam.To understand the math of why it melts at 0°C, imagine a tug-of-war between two natural forces inside the ice: 	1.	Team Enthalpy (The Chemical Bonds): This force wants everything to stay locked, neat, and frozen in a solid structure. It takes energy to break this force. 	2.	Team Entropy (The Desire for Freedom): This force wants the molecules to break free, move around, and become a messy liquid. Temperature (‭$T$‬) acts like a cheerleader for Team Freedom. The higher the temperature, the stronger Team Freedom gets. The Simple Formula Nature decides who wins using a master formula called Gibbs Free Energy (‭$\Delta G$‬‭‬): ‭$$\Delta G = \text{Bond Strength} - (\text{Temperature} \times \text{Freedom})$$‬‭‬‭‬‭‬‭‬‭‬‭‬‭‬ •	If the answer is positive, Team Bonds wins (the water stays frozen as ice). •	If the answer is negative, Team Freedom wins (the ice melts).
content.examTip: Always double-check your specific temperatures for water.

content.question: Water at 100°C has more heat than steam at 100°C.
content.answer: False. Steam contains extra energy (latent heat) that was used to turn liquid into gas. Steam burns are more severe than hot water burns.Mathematically, steam at 100°C contains over 5 times more total heat energy than liquid water at the same temperature. This extra ‭$2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$‬‭‬ of latent energy is released directly onto the skin when steam condenses back into liquid during a burn, making steam burns substantially more severe.
content.teacherTip: Steam is water "plus" the extra energy it needed to escape the liquid.
content.examTip: Steam at 100°C always has MORE heat energy than water at the same temperature.

content.question: Evaporation of a liquid causes cooling.
content.answer: True. As fast-moving (hot) molecules leave the surface, the average energy of the remaining liquid drops.
content.teacherTip: This is why you feel a chill when you step out of a swimming pool.
content.examTip: Explain that heat is "absorbed" from the surroundings.

content.question: Water evaporates only at 100°C.
content.answer: False. Evaporation happens at all temperatures. Boiling specifically requires 100°C.
content.teacherTip: Puddles dry up after rain even if the sun isn't boiling the water!
content.examTip: Use "at all temperatures" to describe evaporation.

content.question: Boiling takes place at all temperatures.
content.answer: False. Boiling only happens at the specific boiling point temperature.
content.teacherTip: Boiling is a "picky" process that waits for the exact right temperature.
content.examTip: Contrast boiling with evaporation.

content.question: Evaporation takes place over the entire mass of the liquid.
content.answer: False. Evaporation is a surface phenomenon — only molecules at the very top escape. Boiling occurs throughout the entire bulk.
content.teacherTip: Evaporation is like a "top-down" exit.
content.examTip: Use the term "surface phenomenon" for evaporation.

content.question: The process of a gas converting directly into solid is called vaporization.
content.answer: False. Gas converting directly into solid is called deposition. Vaporization is liquid to gas.
content.teacherTip: Vaporization creates "vapour" (gas); Deposition "deposits" a solid.
content.examTip: Learn the cycle of change of state carefully.

content.question: At high altitudes water boils above 100°C.
content.answer: False. At high altitudes, air pressure is lower, so water boils at a LOWER temperature.
content.teacherTip: Less air "pushing down" on the water means it can boil with less heat.
content.examTip: Higher altitude = Lower boiling point.

content.question: The melting point of ice is 0°C.
content.answer: True. This is the standard temperature where solid water begins to turn into liquid water.
content.teacherTip: This is the same temperature as the freezing point of water.
content.examTip: Use 0°C as your reference point for water phase questions.

content.question: Evaporation takes place at ______ temperature.
content.answer: Evaporation takes place at ALL temperatures.
content.teacherTip: If you leave a glass of water out, it will eventually disappear.
content.examTip: The keyword here is "all" temperatures.

content.question: ______ process is just reverse of melting.
content.answer: Freezing process is just reverse of melting.
content.teacherTip: Think of melting and freezing as a two-way street.
content.examTip: Use the word "reverse" to show the relationship.

content.question: ______ is a process that involves direct conversion of a solid into its vapour on heating.
content.answer: Sublimation is a process that involves direct conversion of a solid into its vapour on heating.
content.teacherTip: Dry ice is the most famous example.
content.examTip: "Direct conversion" is the key part.

content.question: The temperature at which a solid converts into a liquid is called its ______.
content.answer: The temperature at which a solid converts into a liquid is called its melting point.
content.teacherTip: The "Point" refers to a specific number on the thermometer.
content.examTip: Always include the word "point" after "melting."

content.question: The smallest unit of matter that exists freely in nature is called ______.
content.answer: The smallest unit of matter that exists freely in nature is called molecule.
content.teacherTip: A molecule is like a complete sentence, while atoms are individual letters.
content.examTip: If the question mentions "exists freely," the answer is almost always "molecule."

content.question: Molecules of a substance are always in a state of motion and so they possess ______.
content.answer: Molecules of a substance are always in a state of motion and so they possess kinetic energy.
content.teacherTip: More heat = More motion = More kinetic energy.
content.examTip: Connect "motion" directly to "kinetic energy."

content.question: Intermolecular space is maximum in ______, less in liquids and the least in solids.
content.answer: Intermolecular space is maximum in gases, less in liquids and the least in solids.
content.teacherTip: Space and Force are opposites: More space = Weaker force.
content.examTip: Use "maximum," "less," and "least" to compare the three states.

content.question: Intermolecular force of attraction is maximum in ______, less in liquids and the least in gases.
content.answer: Intermolecular force of attraction is maximum in solids, less in liquids and the least in gases.
content.teacherTip: This force is the "magnetism" that tries to pull molecules together.
content.examTip: This is the most common reason for why solids have a definite shape.

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: Molecules→matter, 100°C→water boils, 0°C→water freezes, At all temperatures→evaporation, Camphor→changes from solid to gas
content.teacherTip: Match the process to its specific temperature or property.
content.examTip: Don't swap boiling and freezing temperatures.

content.question: The inter-molecular force is maximum in:
content.answer: Solids have the strongest forces, which is why they don't change shape.
content.teacherTip: If the force wasn't maximum, the solid would melt or flow away!
content.examTip: Solids are the only state with a truly "maximum" attractive force.

content.question: The inter-molecular space is maximum in:
content.answer: In gases, molecules are very far apart, occupying only a tiny fraction of the total volume.
content.teacherTip: Think of how much space there is in air compared to a block of wood.
content.examTip: Space and Force are always inverse.

content.question: The molecules can move freely anywhere in:
content.answer: Gas molecules are not bound by strong forces, so they can travel freely in straight lines.
content.teacherTip: Molecules in gases are like free-roaming birds.
content.examTip: Focus on the word "anywhere."

content.question: The molecules move only within the boundary of:
content.answer: Liquid molecules can slide around but can't fly away like gas molecules. They stay within the liquid's boundary.
content.teacherTip: Liquid molecules are like people dancing in a ballroom.
content.examTip: Use "boundary" as a clue for liquids.

content.question: The temperature at which a liquid gets converted into its vapour state is called its:
content.answer: The boiling point is where a liquid rapidly turns into gas throughout its entire volume. For water, this is 100°C at sea level.
content.teacherTip: Boiling is the "loud" version of vaporization.
content.examTip: "Liquid to Vapour" at a fixed temperature → boiling point.

content.question: Rapid conversion of water into steam is an example of:
content.answer: Rapid conversion specifically describes vaporization. Unlike evaporation which is slow, vaporization is fast and energetic.
content.teacherTip: Steam is the result of adding lots of heat quickly.
content.examTip: The word "Rapid" is your biggest clue.

content.question: Evaporation takes place from the:
content.answer: Only molecules at the top have the chance to escape into the air.
content.teacherTip: Evaporation is a "top-layer" job.
content.examTip: Always associate "surface" with "evaporation."

content.question: Boiling takes place from the:
content.answer: In boiling, bubbles of gas form even at the bottom and rise up. Energy is high enough throughout the liquid.
content.teacherTip: You can see bubbles forming "throughout" the water.
content.examTip: Use "bulk phenomenon" to define boiling.

content.question: Define the term matter. What is it composed of?
content.answer: Anything which occupies space and has mass is called matter. Matter is composed of tiny particles called MOLECULES.
content.teacherTip: If you can weigh it and it takes up room, it's matter.
content.examTip: Include both "mass" and "space" for a perfect definition.

content.question: State three properties of molecules of a matter.
content.answer:
1. They are very small in size.
2. They have spaces between them.
3. They are in constant motion and they possess kinetic energy.
content.teacherTip: Remember the 3 S's: Small, Spaces, Spinning (motion).
content.examTip: Mentioning "kinetic energy" shows you understand the physics.

content.question: What do you mean by the inter-molecular spaces? How do they vary in different states of matter?
content.answer:
INTER-MOLECULAR SPACES: "The spacing between the molecules of matter is called Inter-molecular spaces." The inter-molecular spaces is less in solids, more in liquids and still more in gases.

Experiment: Take water in a measuring cylinder up to 80 ml mark. Add 10 gm of salt. Volume remains same at 80 ml because salt molecules occupy the spaces between water molecules.
content.teacherTip: Think of molecules in a solid like people on a crowded train, and in a gas like people in a park.
content.examTip: Use the salt experiment as a perfect example to prove that these spaces exist.

content.question: What is meant by the inter-molecular forces of attraction? How do they vary in solids, liquids and gases?
content.answer: INTER-MOLECULAR FORCES OF ATTRACTION: "The forces of attraction between the molecules of matter is called the inter-molecular force of attraction." This inter molecular force is maximum in solids, less in liquids and least in gases.
content.teacherTip: Think of this force as "molecular magnets" pulling on each other.
content.examTip: Use "maximum," "less," and "least" for a clear comparison.

content.question: Discuss the three states of matter (solid, liquid, gas) on the basis of molecular model.
content.answer:
SOLIDS: Molecules are very tightly packed with very less intermolecular space and high intermolecular force. They vibrate about their mean position. Definite shape and volume.

LIQUIDS: Molecules are less tightly packed. Lesser force of intermolecular attraction. Greater intermolecular distance than solids. No definite shape but have definite volume at a given temperature.

GASES: Molecules are far apart with greatest intermolecular distance and weakest intermolecular forces. Molecules move about freely. No definite shape or volume.
content.teacherTip: Draw a simple box with dots for each state.
content.examTip: Use terms like "mean position" for solids.

content.question: What do you mean by the change of state? Write the flow chart showing the complete cycle.
content.answer:
CHANGE OF STATE: "The process of change from one state to another either by absorption or rejection of heat at a constant temperature."

Complete Cycle:
SOLID →(Melting)→ LIQUID →(Vaporization)→ GAS
GAS →(Condensation)→ LIQUID →(Freezing)→ SOLID
SOLID →(Sublimation)→ GAS
GAS →(Deposition)→ SOLID
content.teacherTip: Absorption of heat makes things "looser," rejection makes them "tighter."
content.examTip: Practice drawing the cycle with arrows.

content.question: Differentiate between melting point and boiling point, giving at least one example of each.
content.answer:
MELTING POINT: The temperature at which a solid starts changing into liquid without further increase in temperature. Example: Ice melts at 0°C into water.

BOILING POINT: The temperature at which a liquid starts changing into vapour without further rise in temperature. Example: Water boils at 100°C.
content.teacherTip: Melting point = Solid's "breaking point"; Boiling point = Liquid's "escape point."
content.examTip: Always mention that the temperature remains "constant."

content.question: Describe the process of condensation and sublimation with examples.
content.answer:
CONDENSATION: The change of vapours on cooling at fixed temperature to liquid. Example: Steam at 100°C condenses to water at 100°C on cooling.

SUBLIMATION: The process of change of solid directly into vapours on heating. Example: Naphthalene balls undergo sublimation when exposed to air. Dry ice converts from solid to gas.
content.teacherTip: Condensation is "gas to liquid," Sublimation is "solid to gas."
content.examTip: Mention "cooling" for condensation and "heating" for sublimation.

content.question: What is evaporation? State three factors which affect the rate of evaporation.
content.answer:
EVAPORATION: The change of state of a liquid to vapour at all temperatures from the surface of liquid.

Factors affecting rate of evaporation:
1. Area of exposed surface - more area = faster evaporation
2. Temperature of liquid - higher temperature = faster evaporation
3. Nature of the liquid - volatile liquids evaporate faster
4. Presence of humidity - less humidity = faster evaporation
content.teacherTip: Think of "Hot, Dry, and Wide" for fast evaporation.
content.examTip: Mentioning "Area of exposed surface" is very common for full marks.

content.question: Why is cooling produced on evaporation of a liquid?
content.answer: For evaporation, a liquid requires HEAT. This heat is taken from the surroundings (body, palm, forehead). The temperature of the surroundings falls and we feel cool. The fast-moving (hot) molecules leave the surface, so the average energy of remaining liquid drops.
content.teacherTip: Evaporation is like a "heat thief" that takes warmth away.
content.examTip: Mention that heat is "absorbed from the surroundings."

content.question: Wet clothes dry more quickly on a warm dry day than on a cold humid day. Explain.
content.answer: Rate of evaporation is directly proportional to temperature. On a warm day (higher temperature), rate of evaporation is higher so clothes dry faster. Also, dry air can absorb more moisture, while humid air is already "full" of water vapour.
content.teacherTip: Warm air acts like a "hungry sponge" for water molecules.
content.examTip: Mention "directly proportional to temperature."

content.question: Why does the size of naphthalene balls decrease when left open?
content.answer: When naphthalene balls are left open, due to sublimation they change to vapours directly (without becoming liquid first) and their size decreases. This is why you can smell them even though they are solid.
content.teacherTip: The smell of mothballs is actually the "lost" solid molecules floating in the air.
content.examTip: Use the word "sublimation" as the central reason.

content.question: Why are volatile liquids such as alcohol and spirit stored in tightly closed bottles?
content.answer: Volatile liquids like alcohol and spirit evaporate very easily at room temperature because the intermolecular forces between their molecules are very weak. They are stored in tightly closed bottles to prevent evaporation and loss of the liquid.
content.teacherTip: "Volatile" means it "wants" to be a gas.
content.examTip: Define volatile liquids as those that "evaporate easily at room temperature."

content.question: Why in hot summer days water remains cool in earthen pots?
content.answer: Water seeps out through the pores in the earthen pot and it evaporates. The latent heat required for evaporation is taken from the water inside the pot, which gets cooled. The earthen pot acts like a "sweating" skin for the water inside.
content.teacherTip: Earthen pots are "living" coolers because of their tiny pores.
content.examTip: Mention "pores" and "latent heat" for a complete scientific explanation.

content.question: A patient suffering from high fever is advised to put wet cloth strips on his forehead. Why?
content.answer: Water in wet strips evaporates, taking latent heat required for evaporation from the forehead. The temperature of the forehead (body) falls and the patient feels relieved. This is a simple way to lower body temperature without medicine.
content.teacherTip: The wet cloth acts like a "heat magnet" that carries fever away as it dries.
content.examTip: Focus on the "transfer of heat" from the body to the evaporating water.

content.question: Describe an experiment to demonstrate the process of sublimation.
content.answer: Take some Ammonium chloride powder in a china dish. Cover the china dish with inverted funnel and put a cotton plug in end of funnel so that vapours do not escape. Heat the dish with burner. Solid ammonium chloride changes into vapours. When these vapours come in contact with the cold walls of the funnel, they get cooled and change back to solid (deposition), getting deposited on the funnel walls.
content.teacherTip: The "cotton plug" is essential so you don't lose vapours.
content.examTip: Draw the funnel with the "cotton plug" clearly labeled.

content.content: MASS is the quantity of matter contained in a body. VOLUME is the space occupied by a body.
content.explanation: Mass tells us how much "stuff" is inside an object and stays the same no matter where the object is. Volume describes the three-dimensional room or capacity that the object takes up.
content.teacherTip: Think of Mass as the number of bricks in a pile and Volume as the size of the box those bricks fit into.
content.examTip: For full marks, always define Mass using "quantity of matter" and Volume using "space occupied."

content.content: Equal mass of IRON and cotton — iron will have less volume than cotton because iron is denser.
content.explanation: Because iron is much denser, its particles are packed very tightly, so you only need a small piece to reach a certain mass. Cotton is very loose and airy, so you need a giant pile to weigh the same.
content.teacherTip: Heavy materials take up less room for the same weight.
content.examTip: Mention "density" when explaining why volumes differ for equal masses.

content.content: Equal volume of Iron and cotton — the mass of iron is more than mass of cotton, because iron is denser than cotton.
content.explanation: If you have two boxes of the exact same size, the one filled with iron will be much heavier than the one with cotton. Iron has more matter squeezed into the same space.
content.teacherTip: "Denser" means more crowded particles inside the same size box.
content.examTip: Use the term "denser" specifically to compare the mass of two equal volumes.

content.content: DENSITY is the ratio of mass of substance to volume of substance. D = M/V. The S.I. unit of density is kg m⁻³.
content.explanation: Density is a mathematical way to describe how compact a material is by dividing its mass by its volume. It helps scientists identify substances because every pure material has its own unique density.
content.teacherTip: Use the "Heart" trick: draw an M over a V, like a triangle — M on top, D×V on the bottom.
content.examTip: Always write the formula D = M/V before solving numerical density problems.

content.subtopic: Density Does Not Change with Shape
content.content: Density of a substance does not change with change in shape or size.
content.explanation: Whether you have a tiny gold ring or a large gold bar, the density of the gold remains exactly the same. Density is a property of the material itself, not the object's specific dimensions.
content.teacherTip: A drop of water has the same density as a whole bucket of water.
content.examTip: Remember that cutting an object in half does not change its density.

content.content: When a substance is heated it expands and volume increases, hence density decreases. Water has maximum density at 4°C — density increases from 0°C to 4°C and decreases above 4°C.
content.explanation: Heating makes particles move further apart, taking up more space and making the material less dense. Water is unique because it actually gets denser as it warms up to 4°C before expanding like other liquids.
content.teacherTip: 4°C is the "Magic Number" for water's highest density.
content.examTip: Mention "Anomalous expansion" when talking about why water density is highest at 4°C.

content.content: Volume of substance is measured by formula V = L × B × H (for regular shapes) or by measuring cylinder (for irregular shapes and liquids).
content.explanation: Regularly shaped objects like boxes use multiplication of their sides, while liquids or irregular shapes are measured using a marked container.
content.teacherTip: For a cylinder, always read the bottom of the curved water line (the meniscus).
content.examTip: Units for volume must always be cubed, such as cm³ or m³.

content.content: Mass is measured by beam balance or spring balance.
content.explanation: A beam balance compares an object's mass to standard weights to find a match, while a spring balance measures the pull of gravity.
content.teacherTip: Beam balance measures "Mass," while spring balance technically measures "Weight."
content.examTip: Name the "Beam Balance" specifically when asked for a tool that measures mass accurately.

content.content: RELATIVE DENSITY of substance is the density compared with water — how many times the substance is denser than water. Since density of water is 1 g cm⁻³, density of a substance in g cm⁻³ = relative density. S.I. unit of R.D. has no units since it is the ratio of same quantities.
content.explanation: Relative density is just a number that tells us if something is heavier or lighter than water. Because we divide two identical types of measurements, the units cancel out.
content.teacherTip: Relative Density is a "Comparison Number" with no units.
content.examTip: Never write units like g or cm after a relative density value.

content.content: If a substance has density MORE than liquid it SINKS. If density is LESS than liquid it FLOATS on the liquid.
content.explanation: Heavy, dense objects like stones overcome the upward push of water and fall to the bottom. Lightweight, less dense objects like wood are pushed up by the water and stay on the surface.
content.teacherTip: Dense = Sinker, Less Dense = Floater.
content.examTip: If an object's R.D. is greater than 1, it will sink in water.

content.content: BUOYANT FORCE: The force exerted by liquid acting vertically upward on a body, equal to the weight of liquid displaced by its immersed part. Also called Upthrust.
content.explanation: This is the invisible "upward push" you feel when you try to push a ball under water. The strength depends on how much water the object pushes out of the way.
content.teacherTip: Buoyant force is the "water's helping hand" pushing things up.
content.examTip: Use the term "Upthrust" as another name for Buoyant Force in your answers.

content.content: Weight of body acts vertically downward (tendency to sink). Buoyant force acts vertically upward. The body sinks, floats, or stays suspended depending on which force is greater.
content.explanation: Gravity pulls every object toward the center of the Earth (weight). The liquid pushes back upward (buoyancy). The result depends on which force wins.
content.teacherTip: Weight pulls "Down," Buoyancy pushes "Up."
content.examTip: In a diagram, represent weight with an arrow pointing straight down and buoyancy pointing straight up.

content.content: LAW OF FLOATATION: When a body floats in a liquid, the weight of the liquid displaced by its immersed part is equal to the total weight of the body. Apparent weight of floating body is zero.
content.explanation: When an object is perfectly floating, the water's upward push is exactly as strong as the object's downward pull. Because these forces balance out, the object feels weightless.
content.teacherTip: Floating is a "Tie" between weight and buoyancy.
content.examTip: State that the "Apparent weight" of a floating body is zero.

content.content: Density of body > density of liquid → body SINKS. Density of body = density of liquid → body floats wherever left (neutral buoyancy). Density of body < density of liquid → body RISES and FLOATS on surface.
content.explanation: These three cases describe every possible outcome when you place an object in a liquid. The density comparison is the key to predicting what will happen.
content.teacherTip: Think of a submarine staying perfectly still in the middle of the ocean for neutral buoyancy.
content.examTip: Use "Neutral Buoyancy" to describe the state where an object neither sinks nor rises.

content.question: Equal volumes of two different substances have equal masses.
content.answer: False. Equal volumes of two different substances have different masses because every substance has a unique density.
content.teacherTip: Size (volume) doesn't always equal weight (mass).
content.examTip: If a statement is false, always write the correct version.

content.question: The density of a piece of brass will change by changing its size or shape.
content.answer: False. Density is a property of the material itself, not the object's dimensions. It remains the same regardless of size or shape.
content.teacherTip: Material determines density, not the shape.
content.examTip: Density is constant for a pure substance at constant temperature.

content.question: The density of a liquid decreases with increase in its temperature.
content.answer: True. As a liquid gets hotter, its particles move apart and it expands (volume increases). Since mass stays the same but volume increases, density goes down.
content.teacherTip: Heat expands things, and expansion makes them less dense.
content.examTip: Mention that volume increases when temperature increases.

content.question: Relative density of water is 1.0.
content.answer: True. Relative density is the ratio of a substance's density to water's density. Water compared to itself is exactly 1.
content.teacherTip: Water is our standard "ruler" for relative density.
content.examTip: Use 1.0 as a reference point for all R.D. problems.

content.question: Relative density of a substance is expressed in g cm⁻³.
content.answer: False. Relative density has no units. It is a ratio of two identical physical quantities whose units cancel out.
content.teacherTip: "Relative" means it's just a comparison number.
content.examTip: Never write units for Relative Density.

content.question: When a body is immersed in a liquid, the buoyant force experienced is equal to the volume of the liquid displaced by it.
content.answer: False. The buoyant force is equal to the WEIGHT of the liquid displaced, not the volume.
content.teacherTip: Force = Weight, not Volume.
content.examTip: Carefully distinguish between "Volume" and "Weight" when defining Buoyant Force.

content.question: A body experiences the same buoyant force while floating in water or alcohol.
content.answer: True. When an object floats, the buoyant force must exactly balance the object's weight. Since the object's weight is the same, the upward push is the same in any liquid.
content.teacherTip: Floating is a "Perfect Balance" regardless of the liquid.
content.examTip: The buoyant force on a floating body always equals its own weight.

content.question: A body experiences the same buoyant force when it floats or sinks in water.
content.answer: False. When sinking, the water cannot provide enough buoyant force to match the object's weight. The upthrust differs between floating and sinking.
content.teacherTip: Sinking means the water "lost the battle" to push it up.
content.examTip: Buoyant force changes based on how much of the object is underwater.

content.question: A body floats in a liquid when its weight becomes equal to the weight of the liquid displaced by its submerged part.
content.answer: True. This is the Law of Floatation — the downward pull and upward push are perfectly balanced.
content.teacherTip: This is the Law of Floatation in action.
content.examTip: Use this principle to explain why iron ships can float.

content.question: A body while floating, sinks deeper in a liquid of low density than in a liquid of high density.
content.answer: True. In a low-density liquid, the object must push more liquid aside to get enough upward force. In high-density liquid, the push is stronger so the object doesn't go down as far.
content.teacherTip: It is easier to float in salty sea water than in fresh pool water.
content.examTip: Displacement depends on the density of the liquid.

content.question: 1 kg is the mass of ______ ml of water at 4°C.
content.answer: 1 kg is the mass of 1000 ml of water at 4°C.
content.teacherTip: 1 Litre = 1000 ml = 1 kg of water.
content.examTip: Always mention 4°C if the question asks for the condition of water density.

content.question: Mass = ______ × volume.
content.answer: Mass = density × volume.
content.teacherTip: M = D × V is the "Magic Formula."
content.examTip: Rearrange the formula to D = M/V or V = M/D depending on what you need.

content.question: The S.I. unit of density is ______.
content.answer: The S.I. unit of density is kg m⁻³.
content.teacherTip: kg m⁻³ is just a shorter way to write kg per cubic meter.
content.examTip: Use the correct S.I. unit unless the question specifically asks for C.G.S. units.

content.question: Density of water is ______ kg m⁻³.
content.answer: Density of water is 1000 kg m⁻³.
content.teacherTip: Water is 1 in small units (g/cm³) and 1000 in big units (kg/m³).
content.examTip: Use 1000 as the value for water density when working with S.I. units.

content.question: 1 g cm⁻³ = ______ kg m⁻³.
content.answer: 1 g cm⁻³ = 1000 kg m⁻³.
content.teacherTip: To turn g/cm³ to kg/m³, just multiply by 1000.
content.examTip: Memorize this conversion factor for solving numerical problems quickly.

content.question: The density of a body which sinks in water is more than ______ kg m⁻³.
content.answer: The density of a body which sinks in water is more than 1000 kg m⁻³.
content.teacherTip: If Density > 1000 kg/m³, it goes "Down."
content.examTip: Compare numerical density values to water's density to predict sinking.

content.question: A body sinks in liquid A but floats in liquid B. The density of liquid A is ______ than density of liquid B.
content.answer: The density of liquid A is less than the density of liquid B.
content.teacherTip: Denser liquids are better "supporters."
content.examTip: Floating depends on the relationship between object and liquid density.

content.question: A body X sinks in water but body Y floats. The density of body X is ______ than the density of body Y.
content.answer: The density of body X is more than the density of body Y.
content.teacherTip: Compact objects sink; airy or light objects float.
content.examTip: Use "Sink" and "Float" as clues to rank densities.

content.question: The buoyant force experienced by a body when floating in salt-water is ______ to that when floating in pure water.
content.answer: The buoyant force is equal (same) in both cases because it must match the body's weight.
content.teacherTip: Floating is a "Tie" between Weight and Buoyancy, no matter the liquid.
content.examTip: While the force is the same, the volume of liquid displaced will be different.

content.question: The apparent weight of a body floating in a liquid is ______.
content.answer: The apparent weight of a body floating in a liquid is zero.
content.teacherTip: Think of how light you feel when floating in a swimming pool.
content.examTip: Always specify "apparent weight" if the question asks about weight while floating.

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: kg m⁻³→density, no unit→relative density, relative density→density bottle, iron→sinks in alcohol, wood→floats on water
content.teacherTip: Match units first to eliminate wrong choices.
content.examTip: Draw straight lines or write the correct letter clearly.

content.question: The correct relation is:
content.answer: Mass = Density × Volume. This is derived from D = M/V. Rearranging gives M = D × V.
content.teacherTip: Remember the D = M/V triangle to get all relations correct.
content.examTip: Double-check that your formula makes logical sense before calculating.

content.question: The relative density of alcohol is 0.8. Its density is:
content.answer: R.D. of 0.8 means the substance is 80% as dense as water. Since water is 1000 kg/m³, density = 0.8 × 1000 = 800 kg/m³.
content.teacherTip: R.D. is just the "Decimal Version" of the big density number.
content.examTip: Pay attention to the units; 0.8 is R.D., but the question asks for density.

content.question: A block of wood of density 0.8 g cm⁻³ has a volume of 60 cm³. The mass of block is:
content.answer: Mass = Density × Volume = 0.8 × 60 = 48 g.
content.teacherTip: Always check if your units "match" before you multiply.
content.examTip: Show the step 0.8 × 60 = 48 to get partial marks.

content.question: The density of aluminium is 2.7 g cm⁻³ and that of brass is 8.4 g cm⁻³. The correct statement is:
options: Equal masses of aluminium and brass have equal volumes
The mass of a certain volume of brass is more than the mass of equal volume of aluminium
The volume of a certain mass of brass is more than the volume of equal mass of aluminium
Equal volumes of aluminium and brass have equal masses
content.answer: Since brass has much higher density, it has more matter packed into the same space. A brass block is much heavier than an identically sized aluminum block.
content.teacherTip: Dense things are "heavy for their size."
content.examTip: Compare "Equal Volume" to "Density" to find the heaviest mass.

content.question: A density bottle has a marking 25 mL on it. It means that:
options: the mass of density bottle is 25 g
the density bottle will store 25 ml of any liquid in it
the density bottle will store 25 ml of water, but more of denser liquid
the density bottle will store 25 ml of water, but more of lighter liquid
content.answer: A density bottle holds a very precise fixed volume regardless of what liquid is inside. The marking tells the internal capacity.
content.teacherTip: Volume is about "Capacity," which stays the same even if the liquid changes.
content.examTip: Don't confuse the bottle's volume with the liquid's mass.

content.question: The correct statement is:
options: Buoyant force equals the volume of liquid displaced
Buoyant force equals the volume of the body
Buoyant force equals the weight of liquid displaced
Buoyant force is always equal to the weight of the body
content.answer: This is Archimedes' Principle — the buoyant force equals the weight of the liquid displaced by the immersed part.
content.teacherTip: Buoyancy = Weight of pushed water.
content.examTip: Memorize this exact phrase as it is the most common definition of Buoyancy.

content.question: A piece of wood floats on water. The buoyant force on wood will be:
options: more than the weight of the wood piece
equal to the weight of the wood piece
less than the weight of the wood piece
content.answer: When floating, buoyancy exactly matches weight. If buoyancy were higher, the wood would fly up into the air!
content.teacherTip: Floating is a "Balanced Tug-of-War."
content.examTip: Always state that forces are "equal" when an object is floating steadily.

content.question: The weight of a body is more than the buoyant force experienced by it. The body will:
options: float with some part outside the liquid
float with whole volume above the surface
content.answer: When gravity's pull is stronger than the liquid's upward push, the object is forced downward and sinks.
content.teacherTip: Weight wins the "Downwards" battle, so it sinks.
content.examTip: Draw an arrow diagram with a longer "Weight" arrow to show sinking.

content.question: Define the term density of a substance.
content.answer:
Density of a substance is defined as "Mass per Unit Volume."
Density (d) = Mass / Volume = M/V
S.I. unit: kg m⁻³
C.G.S. unit: g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: Think of density as the "heaviness-per-inch" of a material.
content.examTip: Include the formula d = M/V whenever you define density.

content.question: Name the S.I. unit of density. How is it related to g cm⁻³?
content.answer:
S.I. unit of density is kg m⁻³.
C.G.S. unit is g cm⁻³.

Relationship:
1 kg m⁻³ = 1000 g / (100 cm)³ = 1000 / 1000000 g cm⁻³ = 10⁻³ g cm⁻³
Thus, 1 g cm⁻³ = 1000 kg m⁻³
content.teacherTip: To go from small units to big units, multiply by 1000.
content.examTip: Remember 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ for unit conversion problems.

content.question: The density of brass is 8.4 g cm⁻³. What do you mean by this statement?
content.answer: This means one cubic centimetre volume of brass has a mass of 8.4 g. It describes the exact mass of a standard "cube" of brass that is one centimeter wide on all sides.
content.teacherTip: Statements like this are just "Unit Mass" descriptions.
content.examTip: In your answer, always specify "one cubic unit" and the "mass in grams."

content.question: Arrange the following substances in order of their increasing density: Iron, Cork, Brass, Water, Mercury.
content.answer:
Cork, Water, Iron, Brass, Mercury.

Cork is the lightest (full of air), followed by water (baseline). Mercury is the heaviest (very dense liquid metal).
content.teacherTip: Use "Floaters" and "Sinkers" to help you rank them.
content.examTip: Read carefully whether it asks for "Increasing" or "Decreasing" order.

content.question: How does the density of a liquid (or gas) vary with temperature?
content.answer:
Most liquids increase in volume with increase in temperature, so density decreases.

Water shows anomalous behaviour: Water has maximum density at 4°C. When cooled from high temperature, water contracts up to 4°C (density increases) and expands when cooled further below 4°C (density decreases).

Density of water at 4°C = 1 g cm⁻³ or 1000 kg m⁻³.
content.teacherTip: Heat = Expansion = Lower Density.
content.examTip: Mention that water is "Anomalous" to show you know it behaves differently.

content.question: A given quantity of liquid is heated. Which of the following will vary and how? (a) mass (b) volume (c) density
content.answer:
(a) Mass: does not change.
(b) Volume: increases with rise in temperature.
(c) Density: decreases.

Since D = M/V, when V increases with constant M, density D must decrease.
content.teacherTip: Mass is "Matter" — it stays constant unless you spill some!
content.examTip: Use the formula D = M/V to explain why density drops when volume rises.

content.question: Describe an experiment to determine the density of the material of a coin.
content.answer:
Step 1: Measure mass of coin using beam balance. Let M = 50 g.
Step 2: Take water in measuring cylinder. Note initial volume V₁ = 40 ml.
Step 3: Immerse coin in water. Note final volume V₂ = 50 ml.
Step 4: Volume of coin = V₂ - V₁ = 50 - 40 = 10 ml = 10 cm³
Step 5: Density = M/V = 50/10 = 5 g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: The "Rise in Water" = "Volume of the Coin."
content.examTip: Draw a simple diagram of the measuring cylinder before and after.

content.question: Describe an experiment to determine the density of a liquid.
content.answer:
Step 1: Weigh empty beaker: M₁ = 70 g
Step 2: Fill beaker (half) with milk and weigh: M₂ = 116 g
Step 3: Mass of milk = M₂ - M₁ = 116 - 70 = 46 g
Step 4: Transfer milk to measuring cylinder: V = 40 cc
Step 5: Density = M/V = 46/40 = 1.15 g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: Always remember to subtract the empty container's weight!
content.examTip: Label your masses as M₁ (empty) and M₂ (full) to make calculations clear.

content.question: What is a density bottle? How is it used to find the density of a liquid?
content.answer:
A density bottle is a small glass bottle with a glass stopper at its neck. The stopper has a narrow hole through it. When the bottle is filled with liquid and the stopper is inserted, excess liquid rises through the hole and drains out.

Thus the bottle always contains the same fixed volume of liquid each time. Generally the volume is 25 ml or 50 ml.

It is used by weighing the bottle empty, then full of water, then full of the test liquid — to find density by comparison.
content.teacherTip: The "Hole" in the stopper is the secret to its accuracy.
content.examTip: Mention that the bottle holds a "fixed volume."

content.question: Define the term relative density of a substance.
content.answer:
RELATIVE DENSITY is the ratio of density of a substance to the density of water at 4°C.

OR: The ratio of mass of the substance to the mass of an equal volume of water at 4°C.

It has no units since it is a pure ratio.
content.teacherTip: Relative density is just a "Comparison with Water."
content.examTip: Use the phrase "ratio of densities" for a simple and accurate definition.

content.question: What is the unit of relative density?
content.answer: Relative density has NO UNITS since it is a pure ratio. When we divide density by density, the units cancel out completely, leaving a dimensionless quantity.
content.teacherTip: R.D. is a "Unit-free" zone.
content.examTip: If you write units for Relative Density, you will lose points.

content.question: Distinguish between density and relative density.
content.answer:
DENSITY:
(i) Ratio of mass to volume. D = M/V
(ii) Units are g cm⁻³ or kg m⁻³
(iii) Density in kg m⁻³ = R.D. × 1000

RELATIVE DENSITY:
(i) Ratio of density of substance to density of water
(ii) It is a pure quantity — has no units
(iii) R.D. = Density in g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: Density has "Units," Relative Density has "None."
content.examTip: Create a table with these points to clearly show the differences.

content.question: Explain the meaning of the statement "relative density of aluminium is 2.7".
content.answer: A piece of aluminium of any volume has mass 2.7 times that of an equal volume of water. i.e. Aluminium is 2.7 times heavier than water.
content.teacherTip: 2.7 is the "Heaviness Multiplier" compared to water.
content.examTip: Always mention "equal volume of water" when explaining a relative density value.

content.question: How does the density of a body and that of a liquid determine whether the body will float or sink?
content.answer:
If density of body < density of liquid → body FLOATS on surface.
If density of body > density of liquid → body SINKS in the liquid.
content.teacherTip: High Density = Sinker, Low Density = Floater.
content.examTip: Use a "Greater than" or "Less than" sign to show the density relationship.

content.question: A cork piece floats on water surface while an iron nail sinks in it. Explain the reason.
content.answer:
Cork floats because density of cork is LESS than density of water.
Iron nail sinks because density of iron is MORE than density of water.

Cork is airy and light for its size. Iron is compact metal with heavy particles.
content.teacherTip: It's all about the "Density Match."
content.examTip: Always mention the density of the liquid (water) as the reference point.

content.question: Which will sink or float on water? (a) body A: 500 kg m⁻³ (b) body B: 2520 kg m⁻³ (c) body C: 1100 kg m⁻³ (d) body D: 0.85 g cm⁻³
content.answer:
(a) Body A: 500 kg m⁻³ = 0.5 g cm⁻³ < 1 → FLOATS
(b) Body B: 2520 kg m⁻³ = 2.52 g cm⁻³ > 1 → SINKS
(c) Body C: 1100 kg m⁻³ = 1.1 g cm⁻³ > 1 → SINKS
(d) Body D: 0.85 g cm⁻³ < 1 → FLOATS
content.teacherTip: Turn big S.I. numbers into small decimals by dividing by 1000.
content.examTip: Show your unit conversion step to prove how you reached the float/sink decision.

content.question: What is the law of floatation?
content.answer:
When a body floats in a liquid, the weight of the liquid displaced by its immersed part is equal to the total weight of the body.

Weight of floating body = Weight of liquid displaced by its immersed part.
content.teacherTip: Floating is a "Balanced Weight" act.
content.examTip: Use the mathematical equality Weight = Displaced Liquid Weight.

content.question: For a floating body, how is its weight related to the buoyant force?
content.answer: When a body floats, the weight of the liquid displaced by its immersed part is EQUAL to the total weight of the body. The upward push (buoyant force) and the downward pull (weight) are exactly equal.
content.teacherTip: Weight = Buoyant Force for floating.
content.examTip: Use the word "equal" to describe the relationship for any floating object.

content.question: Why does a piece of ice float on water?
content.answer:
Density of ice (0.9 g cm⁻³) is less than density of water (1 g cm⁻³). Hence, ice floats on water.

When water freezes, its molecules move slightly apart to form a crystal structure, which takes up more space, making ice lighter for its size.
content.teacherTip: Ice is just "puffed up" frozen water.
content.examTip: Mention the numerical densities (0.9 vs 1.0) to support your explanation.

content.question: Explain why an iron needle sinks in water, but a ship made of iron floats on water.
content.answer:
Iron needle: Density of iron > density of water, so it sinks. Weight of iron nail > weight of water displaced.

Iron ship: The ship is HOLLOW and the empty space contains AIR, which makes the AVERAGE DENSITY of ship LESS than water. Also, the ship is shaped to displace MORE weight of water than its own weight.
content.teacherTip: Shape and trapped air are the secrets to making iron float!
content.examTip: Use the term "Average Density" when explaining why hollow ships float.

content.question: It is easier to swim in sea water than in river water. Explain.
content.answer:
Density of sea water is greater than density of river water (because of dissolved salts).

With a smaller portion of body submerged in sea water, the weight of sea water displaced equals the body weight. In river water, a larger portion must be submerged to displace the same weight.

So it is easier to float and swim in sea water.
content.teacherTip: Salt makes water "stronger" at holding you up.
content.examTip: Mention that salt increases the density of water to provide more upthrust.

content.question: Icebergs floating on sea water are dangerous for ships. Explain.
content.answer:
Icebergs are huge masses of ice (density 0.917 g cm⁻³) floating in sea water. About 9/10 of the iceberg is below the water surface and only 1/10 is above.

Captains may see a small piece above water but a giant dangerous mass hides below their ship.
content.teacherTip: The "Tip of the Iceberg" is only 10% of the whole thing!
content.examTip: Use the fraction 9/10 to describe how much is underwater.

content.question: Explain why it is easier to lift a stone under water than in air.
content.answer:
In water, the stone experiences a buoyant force (upthrust) which counterbalances the weight of the stone acting downward. This makes the stone lighter (reduces apparent weight) and thus easier to lift.

In air, you must support its full weight by yourself.
content.teacherTip: Water is your "Invisible Assistant" when lifting things.
content.examTip: Explain that buoyant force reduces the "apparent weight" of the stone.

content.question: What is a submarine? How can it be made to dive and come to the surface?
content.answer:
A submarine is a water-tight boat that can travel under water. It has ballast (water) tanks.

To DIVE: Water is filled into tanks → average density becomes greater than sea water → submarine sinks.

To RISE: Water is pumped out of tanks → average density becomes less than sea water → submarine rises to surface.
content.teacherTip: Submarines use water tanks like "weights" to control their depth.
content.examTip: Mention "Ballast Tanks" as the name of the tanks.

content.question: A balloon filled with hydrogen rises in air. Explain the reason.
content.answer:
Hydrogen is much lighter (less dense) than air, so the balloon displaces more weight of air than the weight of the balloon. The upthrust is greater than the weight, so it rises.

As it rises higher, density of air decreases and upthrust becomes less. Ultimately upthrust equals weight and the balloon stops rising.
content.teacherTip: Just like a cork in water, a light balloon "pops" to the top of the air.
content.examTip: Explain that rising stops when "upthrust equals weight."

content.question: The density of air is 1.28 g/litre. Express it in (a) g cm⁻³ (b) kg m⁻³
content.answer:
(a) 1.28 g/litre = 1.28/1000 g cm⁻³ = 0.00128 g cm⁻³
(b) 1.28 g/litre = 1.28 kg m⁻³

(Since 1 litre = 1000 cm³ and 1 m³ = 1000 litres)
content.teacherTip: Divide by 1000 to go from Litres to cm³.
content.examTip: Show each step of your unit conversion clearly.

content.question: The dimensions of a hall are 10 m × 7 m × 5 m. If the density of air is 1.11 kg m⁻³, find the mass of air in the hall.
content.answer:
Volume V = 10 × 7 × 5 = 350 m³
Density D = 1.11 kg m⁻³
Mass M = V × D = 350 × 1.11 = 388.5 kg
content.teacherTip: Always find "Volume First" in these types of problems.
content.examTip: Don't forget to include the unit kg at the end.

content.question: The density of aluminium is 2.7 g cm⁻³. Express it in kg m⁻³.
content.answer:
Density = 2.7 g/cm³
In kg/m³ = 2.7 × 1000 = 2700 kg/m³
content.teacherTip: Small unit to Big unit = Multiply by 1000.
content.examTip: Memorize that 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³.

content.question: The density of alcohol is 600 kg m⁻³. Express it in g cm⁻³.
content.answer:
Density = 600 kg/m³
In g/cm³ = 600/1000 = 0.60 g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: Big unit to Small unit = Divide by 1000.
content.examTip: Check that your final answer is smaller than the starting number.

content.question: A piece of zinc of mass 438.6 g has a volume of 86 cm³. Calculate the density of zinc.
content.answer:
Mass M = 438.6 g
Volume V = 86 cm³
Density D = M/V = 438.6/86 = 5.1 g/cm³
content.teacherTip: Use long division carefully for decimal masses.
content.examTip: Write out the values for M and V clearly before dividing.

content.question: A piece of wood of mass 150 g has a volume of 200 cm³. Find the density in (a) C.G.S. unit (b) S.I. unit.
content.answer:
(a) D = M/V = 150/200 = 0.75 g/cm³
(b) In S.I. = 0.75 × 1000 = 750 kg/m³

Since 0.75 < 1, this wood will float on water!
content.teacherTip: 0.75 is less than 1, so this wood will float!
content.examTip: Label part (a) and part (b) separately in your answer.

content.question: Calculate the volume of wood of mass 6000 kg if the density of wood is 0.8 g cm⁻³.
content.teacherTip: "Divide for Volume" (M/D).
content.examTip: Always convert to the same unit system before dividing.

content.question: Calculate the density of a solid: Mass = 72 g, Initial water volume = 24 ml, Final volume when solid immersed = 42 ml.
content.answer:
Volume of solid = V₂ - V₁ = 42 - 24 = 18 cm³
Density = M/V = 72/18 = 4.0 g cm⁻³
content.teacherTip: The "Water Rise" is the volume you need.
content.examTip: State the subtraction step 42 - 24 = 18 clearly.

content.question: Mass of empty density bottle = 21.8 g, with water = 41.8 g, with liquid = 40.6 g. Find (a) volume of bottle (b) relative density of liquid.
content.answer:
(a) Mass of water = 41.8 - 21.8 = 20 g
Volume of bottle = 20 cm³ (since 1g water = 1 cm³)

(b) Mass of liquid = 40.6 - 21.8 = 18.8 g
R.D. = Mass of liquid / Mass of water = 18.8/20 = 0.94
content.teacherTip: Grams of water = cm³ of volume.
content.examTip: For R.D., divide the liquid mass by the water mass.

content.question: Mass of empty density bottle = 22 g, with water = 50 g, with brine = 54 g. Calculate density and relative density of brine solution.
content.answer:
Mass of water = 50 - 22 = 28 g
Mass of brine = 54 - 22 = 32 g
Density of brine = 32/28 = 1.14 g/cm³
R.D. of brine = 1.14/1 = 1.14 (no units)
content.teacherTip: Brine is salt water, so it must be denser than 1.
content.examTip: Always subtract M₁ from both M₂ and M₃.

content.question: Mass of empty density bottle = 30 g, with water = 75 g, with liquid = 65 g. Find (a) volume of bottle (b) density of liquid (c) relative density of liquid.
content.answer:
Mass of water = 75 - 30 = 45 g
(a) Volume of bottle = 45 cm³
(b) Mass of liquid = 65 - 30 = 35 g
    Density = 35/45 = 0.77 g/cm³
(c) R.D. = 35/45 = 0.77 (no units)
content.teacherTip: Volume (cm³) = Mass of water (g).
content.examTip: For part (c), remember R.D. has no units.

content.content: FORCE is the cause which changes the state of a body (rest or motion) or changes the size or shape of a body.
content.explanation: Force acts as a push or a pull that can make things move or stop. It can also stretch or squash an object.
content.teacherTip: Think of force as the "Action Hero" that makes objects do something different.
content.examTip: Always mention that force can change both the "state of motion" and the "shape" of an object.

content.content: Weight of a body is the force with which it is attracted towards the centre of earth. W = mg. Mass does not change with location but weight does.
content.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter inside you, which doesn't change on the moon. Since the moon pulls less, you weigh less there even though mass is the same.
content.teacherTip: Mass stays the same everywhere, but weight is a "moody" force that changes with gravity.
content.examTip: Remember the formula W = m × g where m is mass and g is gravity.

content.content: S.I. unit of force is Newton (N). 1 Newton is the force which when acting on a body of mass 1 kg produces a speed of 1 m/s. 1 kgf = 9.8 N ≈ 10 N.
content.explanation: One Newton is roughly the weight of a small apple. It links force directly to mass and acceleration.
content.teacherTip: Multiply kgf by 10 to get approximate Newtons.
content.examTip: Always use capital "N" when writing the unit symbol.

content.content: RIGID body: Force causes only motion (no change in shape). NON-RIGID body: Force causes both motion AND change in size/shape.
content.explanation: A rigid body is like a brick that doesn't bend. A non-rigid body is like a sponge that deforms when squeezed.
content.teacherTip: Rigid = "Super Strong" stays in shape. Non-rigid = "Flexible" like playdough.
content.examTip: For non-rigid bodies, force causes both "motion" and "deformation".

content.content: TURNING EFFECT (Moment of Force) = Force × perpendicular distance from pivot. S.I. unit = N m (Newton meter).
content.explanation: This is the effect when you push a door and it swings on its hinges. The further from the hinge you push, the easier it is to turn.
content.teacherTip: Torque = How hard × How far.
content.examTip: Always check that the distance is "perpendicular" (at 90°) to the force.

content.content: THRUST: Force acting normally (perpendicularly) on a surface. PRESSURE = Thrust/Area = F/A. S.I. unit is pascal (Pa) or N m⁻².
content.explanation: Pressure tells us how spread out a force is. A sharp needle has high pressure because all force is at one tiny point.
content.teacherTip: Pressure is like "Force's concentration".
content.examTip: Mention both N m⁻² and Pascal (Pa) as units of pressure.

content.content: ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE: Pressure exerted by air around us. 1 atm = 76 cm of Hg = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa.
content.explanation: This is the heavy weight of air above us pressing down on everything. We don't feel it because our body pushes back.
content.teacherTip: You are carrying the weight of a small car on your head in air pressure!
content.examTip: Memorize 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa for standard atmospheric pressure.

content.content: Liquid Pressure = h × d × g. Depends on: (i) Height of liquid column (ii) Density of liquid (iii) Gravitational constant. Liquid exerts pressure in ALL directions.
content.explanation: The deeper you dive, the more water above you presses down. Mercury creates more pressure than water because it's denser.
content.teacherTip: Deep = High Pressure. Thick liquid = High Pressure.
content.examTip: Remember P = h × d × g where h is depth, d is density, g is gravity.

content.question: The S.I. unit of force is kgf.
content.answer: False. The S.I. unit of force is Newton (N).
content.teacherTip: S.I. units are the "Science International" standard.
content.examTip: kgf is a gravitational unit, not S.I.

content.question: A force always produces both linear and turning motions.
content.answer: False. A force produces turning motion only if the object is pivoted. Free objects get linear motion only.
content.teacherTip: A push can make a ball roll straight OR make a door swing, but usually not both.
content.examTip: Look for "always" in T/F questions — it often signals false.

content.question: Moment of force = force × perpendicular distance from the pivoted point.
content.answer: True. Both the strength of force and its distance from pivot determine the turning effect.
content.teacherTip: This is why it's easier to open a door by pushing near the handle than near the hinges.
content.examTip: Include "perpendicular" in your definition.

content.question: Less force is needed when applied at a farther distance from the pivoted point.
content.answer: True. Increasing distance increases leverage, creating more turning power with less effort.
content.teacherTip: Distance is your friend when you need to turn something heavy.
content.examTip: This is why spanners have long handles.

content.question: For a given thrust, pressure is more on a surface of large area.
content.answer: False. For a given thrust, pressure is LESS on a large area. P = F/A — larger A means smaller P.
content.teacherTip: Big area = "Comfortable" pressure. Small area = "Stinging" pressure.
content.examTip: Pressure is inversely proportional to Area.

content.question: The S.I. unit of pressure is pascal.
content.answer: True. 1 Pascal = 1 N/m². Named after Blaise Pascal.
content.teacherTip: Pascal is the "Main Name" for pressure units.
content.examTip: Be prepared to define 1 Pascal as 1 N/m².

content.question: A liquid exerts pressure in all directions.
content.answer: True. Unlike solids which only push down, fluids push against anything they contact. Pressure increases with depth.
content.teacherTip: Think of being underwater — water presses on your ears from every side.
content.examTip: This is a fundamental property of fluids.

content.question: Higher we go, greater is the air pressure.
content.answer: False. Air pressure DECREASES as we go higher because the atmosphere becomes thinner.
content.teacherTip: Like a pile of blankets — the higher you are, the fewer blankets on top.
content.examTip: Atmospheric pressure and altitude have an inverse relationship.

content.question: 1 kgf = ______ N (nearly).
content.answer: 1 kgf = 10 N (nearly). Exact value is 9.8 N.
content.teacherTip: "kgf" stands for kilogram-force, roughly ten Newtons.
content.examTip: Use 10 N only when the question says "nearly" or "approximately".

content.question: Moment of force = ______ × distance from the point of turning.
content.answer: Moment of force = force × distance from the point of turning.
content.teacherTip: This is why it's easier to open a heavy gate by pushing at the edge.
content.examTip: Always ensure the distance is measured in metres.

content.question: In a door, handle is provided ______ from the hinges.
content.answer: Handle is placed farthest from the hinges to maximize turning effect with minimum force.
content.teacherTip: Maximum distance = minimum effort for turning.
content.examTip: Use "lever arm" or "perpendicular distance" to explain this design.

content.question: The unit of pressure is ______.
content.answer: The unit of pressure is pascal (Pa).
content.teacherTip: 1 Pa is a very small amount of pressure.
content.examTip: Capitalize "Pascal" but use lowercase "p" for symbol "Pa".

content.question: Pressure in a liquid ______ with the depth.
content.answer: Pressure in a liquid increases with the depth.
content.teacherTip: Like a human pyramid — the person at the bottom feels the most pressure.
content.examTip: Liquid pressure depends on depth (h) and density (d).

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: Camel→broad feet, Truck→six or eight tyres, Knife→sharp cutting edge, Thrust→N, Moment of force→N m, Atmospheric pressure→atm
content.teacherTip: Match biological/structural features to their scientific purpose.
content.examTip: Write full correct pairs clearly.

content.question: S.I. unit of moment of force is:
content.answer: N m. Since moment = Force (N) × Distance (m), the unit is Newton-meter.
content.teacherTip: Moment = Force × Distance, so Unit = Newton × Meter.
content.examTip: Do not confuse N m with N/m.

content.question: The unit of pressure is:
content.answer: N m⁻² (or pascal). Pressure = Force / Area.
content.teacherTip: The ⁻² exponent means "per square meter".
content.examTip: N m⁻² and Pa are identical.

content.question: The pressure and thrust are related as:
content.answer: Pressure = Thrust / Area. The fundamental formula of the chapter.
content.teacherTip: Pressure is the "Average Push" on every tiny bit of surface.
content.examTip: Rearrange to Thrust = Pressure × Area as well.

content.question: The atmospheric pressure at sea level is nearly:
content.answer: 100,000 Pa (10⁵ Pa). This is the standard measurement for sea-level air pressure.
content.teacherTip: 10⁵ is a shorter way to write 100,000.
content.examTip: Be ready to see this as 10⁵ Pa or 100,000 Pa — they're the same.

content.question: Define force. State its S.I. unit.
content.answer: Force is a physical cause that changes or may tend to change the state of rest or the state of motion of an object. The S.I. unit of force is Newton (N).
content.teacherTip: Force = Push or Pull.
content.examTip: Mention "tend to change" to include forces that don't result in actual movement.

content.question: State two effects of a force when applied on a body.
content.answer:
(i) It can change the state of body — stop a moving object or move a stationary object.
(ii) It can change the shape and size of an object.
content.teacherTip: Think "Motion" and "Makeover" (shape change).
content.examTip: Use clear examples like "stopping a ball" to illustrate.

content.question: Define the term moment of force. State its S.I. unit and two factors affecting it.
content.answer:
Moment of force is the turning effect of force acting on a body about an axis.
S.I. unit: N m (Newton meter)
Factors: (i) Magnitude of force applied (ii) Perpendicular distance from axis of rotation.
content.teacherTip: Moment of force is the scientific name for "Turning Power".
content.examTip: Always relate to an "axis" or "pivot point".

content.question: Define pressure. State its S.I. unit and the formula.
content.answer:
Pressure is the thrust (force) acting perpendicularly per unit area of a surface.
P = F/A (Thrust/Area)
S.I. unit: Pascal (Pa) = N m⁻²
content.teacherTip: Pressure is like "Force Density" on a surface.
content.examTip: Include both N m⁻² and Pascal (Pa) as equivalent units.

content.question: State two factors affecting pressure. Give examples.
content.answer:
(i) Area: Greater area = lesser pressure (e.g., camels have broad feet to walk on sand)
(ii) Magnitude of thrust: Greater thrust = greater pressure (e.g., pressing harder with a nail)
Also: Sharp knife cuts easily because small area gives high pressure.
content.teacherTip: Sharp things = Small Area = High Pressure.
content.examTip: Use "inversely proportional" for area and "directly proportional" for thrust.

content.question: State three factors affecting liquid pressure. Give the formula.
content.answer:
Liquid Pressure P = h × d × g
Factors:
(i) Height of liquid column (h) — increases with depth
(ii) Density of liquid (d) — increases with density
(iii) Gravitational constant (g)
content.teacherTip: Deep = High Pressure. Thick liquid = High Pressure.
content.examTip: Remember P = h × d × g.

content.question: Why does nose bleeding occur at high altitudes?
content.answer: At high altitudes, atmospheric pressure decreases. The blood pressure inside our body becomes greater than external atmospheric pressure. This pressure difference causes thin blood vessels in the nose to burst, resulting in nose bleeding.
content.teacherTip: It's like a balloon popping because the air outside got too thin.
content.examTip: Explain that "internal blood pressure" exceeds "external atmospheric pressure".

content.content: WORK is done when force applied on a body causes displacement in the direction of force. W = F × d. S.I. unit is Joule (J). 1 J = 1 N × 1 m.
content.explanation: Work is the transfer of energy through motion. No movement = no work (e.g., pushing a wall).
content.teacherTip: No movement means no work, no matter how much effort you put in!
content.examTip: Always explain that displacement is zero in cases like pushing a wall.

content.content: No work is done when: (i) No displacement (S = 0), e.g., pushing a wall. (ii) Displacement is perpendicular to force (θ = 90°), e.g., coolie walking with box on head.
content.explanation: A coolie with a box on his head walking does no work against gravity — force is vertical, motion is horizontal.
content.teacherTip: Pushing "Up" while moving "Forward" = zero work done on the load.
content.examTip: Mention that the angle between force and displacement is 90°.

content.content: ENERGY is the capacity of doing work. S.I. unit = Joule (J). Same unit as work.
content.explanation: Energy is like a "bank account" that allows objects to perform actions. Because work and energy are the same thing in different forms, they share units.
content.teacherTip: Think of energy as the "fuel" needed to perform work.
content.examTip: Defining energy as "capacity to do work" is the most accurate definition.

content.content:
MECHANICAL ENERGY = Potential Energy + Kinetic Energy.
POTENTIAL ENERGY (P.E.) = mgh — energy due to position/height.
KINETIC ENERGY (K.E.) = ½mv² — energy due to motion.
content.explanation: PE is stored energy (a book on a shelf). KE is movement energy (a rolling ball). They can convert into each other.
content.teacherTip: PE and KE are like two sides of the same mechanical coin.
content.examTip: When a body falls: PE decreases, KE increases. Total remains constant.

content.content: POWER is the rate of doing work. P = W/t. S.I. unit = Watt (W) = J/s. 1 H.P. = 746 W.
content.explanation: Power measures how fast work is being done. A more powerful motor lifts weight faster.
content.teacherTip: Power is just "Work with a stopwatch".
content.examTip: Use P = W/t when given "Work" and "Seconds".

content.question: A coolie does no work against the force of gravity while carrying luggage on a road.
content.answer: True. The motion is horizontal but gravity is vertical — angle is 90° so work against gravity is zero.
content.teacherTip: Work requires force and motion to be in the same line!
content.examTip: Specify angle is 90° between force and motion.

content.question: The energy stored in water of a dam is the kinetic energy.
