content.answer: False. Water in a dam is at a great height — it has gravitational POTENTIAL energy.
content.teacherTip: Static things at a height have "Potential," not Kinetic energy.
content.examTip: If water is "stored" or "still," the answer is Potential Energy.

content.question: Work done by a boy depends on the time in which he does work.
content.answer: False. Work depends only on force and distance, not time. Time is used to calculate Power.
content.teacherTip: Work doesn't care about the clock; Power does!
content.examTip: Do not include "time" in your formula for Work.

content.question: Power spent by a body depends on the time for which it does work.
content.answer: True. Power = Work/Time. Same work in less time = more power.
content.teacherTip: Power is "Speedy Work".
content.examTip: Power is inversely proportional to time for a given amount of work.

content.question: Work done = Force × ______.
content.answer: Work done = Force × distance moved in direction of force.
content.teacherTip: Direction matters! Push and move must be in the same line.
content.examTip: Memorize W = F × d as your primary work formula.

content.question: The S.I. unit of energy is ______.
content.answer: The S.I. unit of energy is joule (J).
content.teacherTip: Joule is the "Global Currency" for energy.
content.examTip: Joule is used for both Work and Energy.

content.question: Kinetic energy = ½ × mass × ______.
content.answer: Kinetic energy = ½ × mass × (speed)². Doubling speed quadruples KE!
content.teacherTip: Speed has a much bigger effect on KE than mass does.
content.examTip: Don't forget "½" and "squared" in the KE formula.

content.question: 1 H.P. = ______ W.
content.teacherTip: 746 is the "Magic Number" to convert H.P. to Watts.
content.examTip: Memorize this exact value for unit conversion questions.

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: Stone at height→PE, Moving ball→KE, Energy→joule, Power→work done in 1s, watt→power
content.teacherTip: Match objects to their "Energy Type" and units to their "Quantities".
content.examTip: Draw clean straight lines for matching.

content.question: The S.I. unit of work is:
content.answer: Joule. Work and energy always use the Joule as their standard unit.
content.teacherTip: Joule = Work.
content.examTip: Be careful not to pick Newton (force) by mistake.

content.question: No work is done by a force if the body:
content.answer: Does not move. If displacement = 0, then F × d = 0.
content.teacherTip: "Static" objects have zero work done on them.
content.examTip: "No work" usually points to "no displacement".

content.question: When a boy doubles his speed, his kinetic energy becomes:
content.answer: Four times. KE = ½mv². Doubling v gives (2v)² = 4v², so KE becomes 4 times.
content.teacherTip: Double speed = Quadruple energy (2 × 2 = 4).
content.examTip: Use the v² part of the formula to explain.

content.question: A boy lifts luggage from height 2 m to 4 m. The potential energy will become:
content.answer: Double. PE is directly proportional to height. 4m is twice 2m.
content.teacherTip: Double height = Double PE.
content.examTip: Heights and PE follow the same ratio if mass is constant.

content.question: Define work. State two conditions when no work is done.
content.answer:
Work is done when a force applied on a body causes displacement in the direction of force. W = F × d.

No work is done when:
(i) Displacement S = 0 (e.g., pushing a wall)
(ii) Displacement is perpendicular to force, θ = 90° (e.g., coolie walking with load on head)
content.teacherTip: Zero move OR Zero angle = Zero Work.
content.examTip: List both conditions separately for full credit.

content.question: Define energy. What are the two kinds of mechanical energy?
content.answer:
ENERGY: Capacity of doing work. S.I. unit = Joule (J).

Two kinds of mechanical energy:
(i) Potential Energy (PE) — energy due to position or shape
(ii) Kinetic Energy (KE) — energy due to motion

Mechanical Energy = PE + KE
content.teacherTip: PE = Place; KE = Kick (motion).
content.examTip: Mention both PE and KE as components of mechanical energy.

content.question: Write expressions for PE and KE, explaining symbols used.
content.answer:
PE = mgh where m = mass, g = acceleration due to gravity, h = height above ground.

KE = ½mv² where m = mass, v = speed/velocity.

PE depends on mass and height. KE depends on mass and velocity (v² makes speed very important).
content.teacherTip: P.E. likes heavy things and high places. K.E. loves speed.
content.examTip: Label each symbol clearly to get full marks.

content.question: Define power. State its S.I. unit. What is 1 H.P.?
content.answer:
Power is the rate of doing work. P = W/t = F × d/t.
S.I. unit: Watt (W) = J/s.
1 H.P. (Horse Power) = 746 W.
content.teacherTip: 1 Watt = 1 Joule per second.
content.examTip: Use P = W/t when given "Work" and "Seconds".

content.question: Name the type of energy: (a) moving cricket ball (b) stone on building top (c) compressed spring (d) stretched rubber band (e) water flowing in river
content.answer:
(a) Moving cricket ball — Kinetic Energy (due to motion)
(b) Stone on building top — Potential Energy (due to height)
(c) Compressed spring — Potential Energy (due to changed shape)
(d) Stretched rubber band — Potential Energy (due to elongated state)
(e) Water flowing in river — Kinetic Energy (due to motion)
content.teacherTip: Motion = Kinetic; Height/Stretching = Potential.
content.examTip: Label the specific reason next to your answer.

content.content: Light is a form of energy that produces sensation of sight. Speed in vacuum/air = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Speed decreases in denser media: water (2.25 × 10⁸), glass (2 × 10⁸). Light travels in straight lines.
content.explanation: Light travels fastest in vacuum and slows down in denser materials like water and glass.
content.teacherTip: As the material gets harder (Gas → Liquid → Solid), light gets slower.
content.examTip: Memorize speed of light in air: 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

content.content: REFRACTION: Change in direction of light when passing from one transparent medium to another. Rarer to Denser → bends TOWARDS normal. Denser to Rarer → bends AWAY from normal.
content.explanation: Light bends because it changes speed at the boundary between two media.
content.teacherTip: Use mnemonic "DRA" — Denser to Rarer = Away from normal.
content.examTip: Both media must be "transparent" for refraction to occur.

content.content:
(i) Incident ray, normal, and refracted ray all lie in the same plane.
(ii) sin∠i / sin∠r = constant = Refractive Index (μ).
R.I. = Speed in air / Speed in medium = Real depth / Apparent depth.
content.explanation: The ratio of sines of angles is constant for any two media. Higher R.I. = slower light = denser medium.
content.teacherTip: R.I. of water = 1.33, glass = 1.5.
content.examTip: Use μ = sin i / sin r for calculations.

content.content: DISPERSION: Splitting of white light into seven colours (VIBGYOR) by a prism. White light is a band of seven colours. In glass: violet is slowest, red is fastest.
content.explanation: Each colour has different speed in glass, so they bend by different amounts and separate.
content.teacherTip: Rainbows are a natural form of dispersion.
content.examTip: Violet bends the most, Red bends the least.

content.content:
CONCAVE mirror: converges light, forms real/virtual images. Used as shaving mirror.
CONVEX mirror: diverges light, always forms virtual, erect, diminished image. Used as rear-view mirror.
Focal length f = R/2 (R = radius of curvature).
content.explanation: Concave mirrors focus light to a point. Convex mirrors spread light out giving a wider field of view.
content.teacherTip: Concave = "cave in" bringing light to center. Convex = "bulge out" spreading light.
content.examTip: f = R/2 is a key formula for mirror calculations.

content.question: Water is optically denser than glass.
content.answer: False. Glass (R.I. = 1.5) is optically denser than water (R.I. = 1.33). Light is slower in glass.
content.teacherTip: Remember: Air (1.0), Water (1.33), Glass (1.5).
content.examTip: Use "optical density" to describe how much a medium slows light.

content.question: A ray of light from glass to air bends towards the normal.
content.answer: False. Glass to air = denser to rarer = bends AWAY from normal.
content.teacherTip: Use mnemonic "DRA" — Denser to Rarer = Away.
content.examTip: Draw the normal perpendicular to the surface.

content.question: The speed of light is more in glass than in water.
content.answer: False. Glass is denser than water. Light in water = 2.25×10⁸, in glass = 2×10⁸ m/s. Slower in glass.
content.teacherTip: Denser materials = "thicker traffic" for light.
content.examTip: Speed is inversely proportional to refractive index.

content.question: The depth of a pond seen from above appears to be less.
content.answer: True. Refraction makes light from the bottom bend away from normal as it enters air, making the bottom appear raised.
content.teacherTip: This is called "Apparent Depth" — pools look shallower than they are.
content.examTip: Use dotted line for virtual position of raised object.

content.question: A convex mirror is used as a shaving mirror.
content.answer: False. CONCAVE mirrors are used as shaving mirrors because they form enlarged, erect images when held close to face.
content.teacherTip: Shaving needs "zoom," so we use concave mirrors.
content.examTip: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors.

content.question: A concave mirror converges light rays, but a convex mirror diverges them.
content.answer: True. Concave mirrors reflect rays inward toward a focus. Convex mirrors reflect rays outward.
content.teacherTip: Converge = Come together; Diverge = Drive apart.
content.examTip: Use arrows to show direction of reflected rays in diagrams.

content.question: Water is optically ______ than air.
content.answer: Water is optically denser than air.
content.teacherTip: Rarer = thinner; Denser = thicker.
content.examTip: Write "optically denser" for physics accuracy.

content.question: When white light passes through a prism, it ______.
content.answer: When white light passes through a prism, it disperses into seven colours (VIBGYOR).
content.teacherTip: A prism is a "color separator."
content.examTip: White light must fall obliquely for dispersion.

content.question: Radius of curvature of a spherical mirror is ______ times its focal length.
content.answer: Radius of curvature = 2 × focal length. R = 2f.
content.teacherTip: R = 2f. Use this to check your math!
content.examTip: State units (cm) when giving values for R or f.

content.question: A ______ mirror always forms a virtual image.
content.answer: A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, diminished image.
content.teacherTip: Convex = "always small and upright" mirror.
content.examTip: Virtual images are always erect and diminished for convex mirrors.

content.question: The speed of light in air or vacuum is:
content.answer: 3 × 10⁸ m/s. This is a fundamental physical constant.
content.teacherTip: That's 300,000 km every single second!
content.examTip: Units must be m/s.

content.question: The seven colours of sunlight in order are:
content.answer: VIBGYOR — Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red.
content.teacherTip: Use "Roy G. Biv" backwards.
content.examTip: Violet bends most, Red bends least.

content.question: If radius of curvature of a concave mirror is 20 cm, its focal length is:
content.teacherTip: Radius is always the "big" number, focal length is the "small" number.
content.examTip: Include the unit (cm) in your answer.

content.question: A convex mirror is used:
options: as a rear view mirror by a driver
content.answer: As a rear view mirror. Convex mirrors give a wide field of view by shrinking images.
content.teacherTip: "Objects in mirror are closer than they appear."
content.examTip: Mention "wide field of view" as the reason.

content.question: State the speed of light in (a) air (b) water (c) glass.
content.answer:
(a) Air: 3 × 10⁸ m/s
(b) Water: 2.25 × 10⁸ m/s
(c) Glass: 2 × 10⁸ m/s

Light slows down in denser media.
content.teacherTip: As material gets denser, light gets slower.
content.examTip: Use 10⁸ scientific notation.

content.question: What is refraction of light? State the laws of refraction.
content.answer:
REFRACTION: Change in direction of path of light when it passes from one transparent medium to another.

Laws of Refraction (Snell's Laws):
(i) Incident ray, normal at point of incidence, and refracted ray all lie in the same plane.
(ii) sin∠i / sin∠r = constant = Refractive Index (μ).
content.teacherTip: Refraction = Bending at the border.
content.examTip: Both media must be "transparent" for refraction.

content.question: What is dispersion of light? Name the seven colours.
content.answer:
DISPERSION: The splitting of white light into its seven constituent colours when passed through a prism.

Seven colours (VIBGYOR): Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red.

Cause: Different colours have different speeds in glass, so they separate on refraction.
content.teacherTip: Rainbows are natural dispersion.
content.examTip: Violet bends most (slowest in glass), Red bends least (fastest in glass).

content.content: Heat is a form of energy. It flows from higher temperature to lower temperature body until both reach same temperature. When heated: (i) body expands (ii) temperature changes (iii) state changes.
content.explanation: Heat energy makes molecules move faster, increasing kinetic energy. When cooling, KE decreases.
content.teacherTip: Heat always flows from hot to cold, never the other way.
content.examTip: Mention all three effects of heating for complete answer.

content.content:
MELTING: Solid → Liquid at constant temperature. Melting Point of ice = 0°C.
BOILING: Liquid → Gas at constant temperature. Boiling Point of water = 100°C.
EVAPORATION: Liquid → Gas at ALL temperatures (surface phenomenon). Produces cooling.
SUBLIMATION: Solid → Gas directly.
content.explanation: Temperature remains constant during change of state. Heat is used to break molecular bonds.
content.teacherTip: Evaporation = slow at surface. Boiling = fast throughout.
content.examTip: Distinguish boiling (fixed temp) from evaporation (all temps).

content.content:
When a metal rod is heated, its length increases. Increase depends on: (i) original length (ii) rise in temperature (iii) material of rod.
Coefficient of linear expansion α = (Lt - L₀)/(L₀ × T).
α : β : γ = 1 : 2 : 3 (linear : superficial : cubical).
content.explanation: Different materials expand at different rates. This is used in bimetallic strips for thermostats.
content.teacherTip: Brass expands more than iron when heated by the same amount.
content.examTip: Remember α : β : γ = 1 : 2 : 3.

content.content: Order of expansion: Gases > Liquids > Solids. Alcohol expands more than water. Iron expands less than copper.
content.explanation: Gases have weakest intermolecular forces, so molecules move apart most easily when heated.
content.teacherTip: Gases are the "expansion champions".
content.examTip: State the order: Gas > Liquid > Solid expansion.

content.question: Evaporation is rapid on a wet day.
content.answer: False. On a wet day, air is saturated with moisture — little room for more water to evaporate. High humidity slows evaporation.
content.teacherTip: High humidity = crowded room where no one else can enter.
content.examTip: Evaporation rate is inversely proportional to humidity.

content.question: Evaporation takes place only from the surface of liquid.
content.answer: True. Evaporation is a surface phenomenon — only molecules at the top escape into air.
content.teacherTip: Like people leaving a stadium — only those near exits can leave first.
content.examTip: Use "surface phenomenon" to describe evaporation.

content.question: Temperature of a liquid rises during boiling.
content.answer: False. Temperature remains CONSTANT during boiling. Heat is used to change state (latent heat), not raise temperature.
content.teacherTip: During "change of state," the thermometer stays still.
content.examTip: Mention "Latent Heat" to explain constant temperature.

content.question: Solids expand the least and gases expand the most on being heated.
content.answer: True. Gases have weakest intermolecular forces, allowing molecules to move far apart easily.
content.teacherTip: Order: Gas > Liquid > Solid expansion.
content.examTip: Mention "weak intermolecular forces" for gases.

content.question: All solids expand by the same amount when heated to the same rise in temperature.
content.answer: False. Different solids have different coefficients of expansion. Brass expands more than iron.
content.teacherTip: Every material has its own "personality" of expansion.
content.examTip: Expansion depends on "nature of the material."

content.question: Boiling occurs at a ______ temperature.
content.answer: Boiling occurs at a fixed temperature (e.g., water boils at 100°C).
content.teacherTip: Boiling is like a scheduled appointment at a specific temperature.
content.examTip: Distinguish boiling (fixed) from evaporation (any temperature).

content.question: Evaporation takes place at ______ temperature.
content.answer: Evaporation takes place at all temperatures.
content.teacherTip: Evaporation is a "24/7" process.
content.examTip: Use "all temperatures" to define evaporation.

content.question: Gases expand ______ than the liquids.
content.answer: Gases expand more than liquids.
content.teacherTip: Gases are the "expansion champions".
content.examTip: Order: Gas > Liquid > Solid.

content.question: Iron expands ______ than copper.
content.answer: Iron expands less than copper. Copper has higher coefficient of linear expansion.
content.teacherTip: Copper "grows" faster than iron.
content.examTip: This is the principle behind bimetallic strips.

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: Blowing air→evaporation, Increase in pressure→boiling point, Thermal expansion→inter-molecular separation, Invar→pendulum, Pyrex glass→cooking utensils
content.teacherTip: Invar has very low expansion — used for precision instruments.
content.examTip: Pyrex glass has low expansion — doesn't crack when heated.

content.question: In evaporation:
content.answer: Only surface molecules escape. Evaporation is a surface phenomenon.
content.teacherTip: "Surface Only" for evaporation, "Whole Bulk" for boiling.
content.examTip: Distinguish surface molecules from bulk molecules.

content.content: SOUND is energy that produces sensation of hearing. Produced by vibration. Needs a medium for propagation — cannot travel in vacuum. Speed: solids (5000 m/s in steel) > liquids (1500 m/s in water) > gases (330 m/s in air).
content.explanation: Sound needs molecules to carry energy. In space (vacuum), there is complete silence.
content.teacherTip: Solids are "fast lanes" for sound waves.
content.examTip: Memorize speed of sound in air ≈ 330-340 m/s.

content.content:
Sound travels as LONGITUDINAL waves (compressions & rarefactions).
AMPLITUDE: Maximum displacement from mean position → determines LOUDNESS.
FREQUENCY (f): Vibrations per second, measured in Hertz (Hz). f = 1/T.
WAVELENGTH (λ): Distance between two consecutive compressions. Unit: metre.
content.explanation: Compressions are areas of high pressure, rarefactions are low pressure areas.
content.teacherTip: Amplitude = "height" of vibration. Frequency = "speed" of vibration.
content.examTip: 1 Hz = 1 vibration per second.

content.content:
Three characteristics: (i) LOUDNESS — depends on amplitude. Unit: decibel (dB). Safe limit: 0-80 dB.
(ii) PITCH — depends on frequency. High frequency = shrill/high pitch.
(iii) QUALITY (Timbre) — depends on waveform. Distinguishes instruments playing same note.
content.explanation: These three properties let us identify different sounds — a whisper vs shout, flute vs piano.
content.teacherTip: Remember "L-A-P-F": Loudness-Amplitude, Pitch-Frequency.
content.examTip: List all three when asked for characteristics of sound.

content.question: When sound propagates in air, it does not carry energy with it.
content.answer: False. Sound IS a form of energy that travels through air by vibrating particles.
content.teacherTip: If sound didn't carry energy, it couldn't move your eardrum.
content.examTip: All waves are transporters of energy.

content.question: In a longitudinal wave, compression and rarefaction are formed.
content.answer: True. Longitudinal waves consist of high-pressure (compression) and low-pressure (rarefaction) regions.
content.teacherTip: Compression = "Squeeze", Rarefaction = "Stretch".
content.examTip: Mention both terms when describing sound waves.

content.question: The frequency is measured in second.
content.answer: False. Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz). Second is the unit for Time Period.
content.teacherTip: Hertz = 1/second.
content.examTip: Frequency is Hz, Time is s.

content.question: The pitch of sound depends on frequency.
content.answer: True. Higher frequency = higher pitch. Pitch and frequency are directly related.
content.teacherTip: Faster vibrations mean higher notes.
content.examTip: Pitch is directly proportional to Frequency.

content.question: Decibel is the unit of pitch of a sound.
content.answer: False. Decibel (dB) is the unit of LOUDNESS. Pitch is related to frequency (Hz).
content.teacherTip: Decibels = "volume", Hertz = "note".
content.examTip: Don't confuse: Loudness (dB) vs Pitch (Hz).

content.question: The time period of a wave is 2 s. Its frequency is ______ Hz.
content.teacherTip: f = 1/T. Simple division.
content.examTip: Show calculation f = 1/T for partial credit.

content.question: A tuning fork produces sound of ______ frequency.
content.answer: A tuning fork produces a pure tone of single frequency.
content.teacherTip: Tuning forks are "standard rulers" for sound frequency.
content.examTip: Use "pure tone" to describe a tuning fork.

content.question: Match Column A with Column B
content.answer: Amplitude→max displacement, Frequency→1/T, Loudness→amplitude, Pitch→frequency, Waveform→other frequencies
content.teacherTip: L-A-P-F: Loudness-Amplitude, Pitch-Frequency.
content.examTip: Cross-check each pair before finalizing.

content.question: Sound cannot travel in:
content.answer: Vacuum. Sound requires a material medium. Space is silent.
content.teacherTip: "In space, no one can hear you scream."
content.examTip: Always choose vacuum as where sound cannot travel.

content.question: The safe limit of loudness of audible sound is:
content.answer: 0 to 80 dB. Sounds above 80 dB can cause hearing damage.
content.teacherTip: Above 80 dB = danger zone for ears.
content.examTip: Memorize 80 dB as the safe limit.

content.question: Define sound. How is it produced?
content.answer:
Sound is a form of energy which produces the sensation of hearing. It is produced by the vibration of a body.

Sound needs a material medium for propagation and cannot travel in vacuum. It travels as longitudinal waves.
content.teacherTip: Without vibration, there is no sound.
content.examTip: Define sound as a form of energy requiring a vibrating source.

content.question: State the three characteristics of sound. What does each depend on?
content.answer:
(i) LOUDNESS — depends on amplitude of wave. Unit: decibel (dB).
(ii) PITCH — depends on frequency. More frequency = high pitch (shrill).
(iii) QUALITY (Timbre) — depends on wave form. Distinguishes different instruments.
content.teacherTip: Big Amplitude = Big Loudness. Fast vibration = High Pitch.
content.examTip: List all three with their dependencies.

content.question: Define: amplitude, frequency, wavelength, time period.
content.answer:
AMPLITUDE: Maximum displacement of particle from mean position.
FREQUENCY: Number of vibrations per second. Unit: Hz. f = 1/T.
WAVELENGTH (λ): Distance between two consecutive compressions (or rarefactions). Unit: metre.
TIME PERIOD (T): Time for one complete vibration. Unit: second. T = 1/f.
content.teacherTip: Frequency and Time Period are inverses of each other.
content.examTip: Include units for each quantity.

content.content:
ELECTRICITY: Rate of flow of electrons. i = q/t. S.I. unit of current: Ampere (A). S.I. unit of charge: Coulomb (C).
POTENTIAL DIFFERENCE (V): Work done per unit charge. V = W/Q. Unit: Volt (V).
Electrical Energy W = VIt.
content.explanation: Current is like water flowing through a pipe. Potential difference is the "push" from a battery.
content.teacherTip: A battery is like a "pump" for electrons.
content.examTip: Always write i = q/t and define each symbol.

content.content:
POWER P = VI = W/t. Unit: Watt (W). 1 W = 1 V × 1 A.
Electrical Energy = VIt. S.I. unit: Joule. Commercial unit: kWh.
1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 1 unit (for electricity bills).
content.explanation: Power tells how quickly an appliance uses energy. kWh is what the electric company uses for billing.
content.teacherTip: Power = "Energy per Second".
content.examTip: Don't confuse kW (power) with kWh (energy).

content.content: Power generated at 11 kV → stepped up to 132 kV at grid sub-station (to minimize energy loss) → stepped down to 33 kV at main sub-station → finally to 220 V for household supply.
content.explanation: High voltage transmission reduces energy loss in wires. Multiple step-downs bring it to safe 220V for homes.
content.teacherTip: Step up for long distance, step down for safety.
content.examTip: Remember sequence: 11 kV → 132 kV → 33 kV → 220 V.

content.content:
EARTHING: Safety device at zero potential — protects from electric shock. Connected to metal body of appliances.
FUSE: Limits current. Has high resistance and low melting point. Connected in LIVE wire.
SHORT CIRCUIT: Live wire touches neutral wire due to damaged insulation.
Colour coding: Live = Red/Brown, Neutral = Black/Blue, Earth = Green/Yellow.
content.explanation: Fuse melts to break circuit when current is too high. MCBs are modern replacements. Appliances are connected in parallel.
content.teacherTip: A fuse "gives its life" to save your expensive TV!
content.examTip: Always list "Low Melting Point" and "High Resistance" for fuse wire.

content.question: A fuse wire has a high melting point.
content.answer: False. A fuse wire has a LOW melting point so it melts quickly when current is too high, breaking the circuit.
content.teacherTip: Fuse = "weak link" designed to break first for safety.
content.examTip: State that melting point is LOW.

content.question: Flow of protons constitutes electric current.
content.answer: False. Electric current is the flow of ELECTRONS. Protons stay in the nucleus.
content.teacherTip: Electrons "run", Protons "stay put".
content.examTip: Define current as "rate of flow of electrons."

content.question: S.I. unit and commercial unit of electrical energy are same.
content.answer: False. S.I. unit is Joule (J). Commercial unit is kilowatt-hour (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.
content.teacherTip: Joules for "Science", kWh for "Bills".
content.examTip: Remember 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

content.question: Overloading of electric current in circuits can lead to short circuiting.
content.answer: True. Overloaded wires get extremely hot, melting insulation. Bare wires may touch causing short circuit.
content.teacherTip: Heat from overloading is a wire's worst enemy.
content.examTip: "Melting of insulation" links overloading to short circuits.

content.question: The earth wire protects us from an electric shock.
content.answer: True. Earth wire provides a safe route for stray electricity to go into the ground instead of through your body.
content.teacherTip: Earth wire = "emergency exit" for electricity.
content.examTip: Earth wire is connected to the "metal body" of the appliance.

content.question: A fuse is connected to the live wire.
content.answer: True. Fuse must be in the live wire so that when it melts, the appliance is fully disconnected from power.
content.teacherTip: Putting fuse in "Live" wire cuts power at the source.
content.examTip: Safety devices (fuse, switch) are always in the Live wire.

content.question: In a cable, the green wire is the live wire.
content.answer: False. Green (or green-yellow) wire is the EARTH wire. Live wire is Red or Brown.
content.teacherTip: "Green is the Ground."
content.examTip: Live = Red/Brown, Earth = Green/Yellow, Neutral = Black/Blue.

content.question: The unit in which we pay for electricity is ______.
content.answer: The commercial unit of electrical energy is kWh (kilowatt-hour). 1 kWh = 1 unit.
content.teacherTip: kWh is the "unit of the bill".
content.examTip: Don't confuse kW (power) with kWh (energy).

content.question: 1 kWh = ______ J.
content.teacherTip: 3.6 million joules in one unit of electricity.
content.examTip: Memorize this conversion factor.

content.question: The colour of the earth wire is ______.
content.answer: Earth wire is Green or Green-Yellow.
content.teacherTip: "Green is the Ground."
content.examTip: Know colour codes: Live=Red/Brown, Neutral=Black/Blue, Earth=Green/Yellow.

content.question: A fuse wire should have high resistance and ______ melting point.
content.answer: A fuse wire should have high resistance and LOW melting point.
content.teacherTip: Low melting point means it melts quickly to break circuit.
content.examTip: Both properties are important for fuse wire.

content.question: The S.I. unit of electric current is:
content.answer: Ampere (A). Current i = q/t, measured in Amperes.
content.teacherTip: "Amp" is short for Ampere.
content.examTip: Remember C-A-V-J-W units.

content.question: 1 kWh is equal to:
content.examTip: Show the calculation: 1000 W × 3600 s.

content.question: The voltage of electricity supplied to our houses is:
content.answer: 220 V. This is the standard safe voltage for household supply.
content.teacherTip: 220 V is the magic number for home electricity.
content.examTip: Know the full chain: 11→132→33 kV→220 V.

content.question: Define electric current. State its S.I. unit.
content.answer:
Electric current is the rate of flow of charge (electrons) through a conductor.
i = q/t where q = charge in coulombs, t = time in seconds.
S.I. unit: Ampere (A).
content.teacherTip: Current = "speed" of the electricity river.
content.examTip: Write formula i = q/t and define each symbol.

content.question: Define potential difference and power. State their units.
content.answer:
POTENTIAL DIFFERENCE: Work done in moving a unit positive charge from one point to another. V = W/Q. Unit: Volt (V).

POWER: Rate of doing work. P = W/t = VI. Unit: Watt (W).
1 Watt = 1 Volt × 1 Ampere.
content.teacherTip: Use the "W-Q-V" triangle to remember formulas.
content.examTip: Ensure time is in seconds for standard formula.

content.question: What is earthing? Why is it done?
content.answer:
Earthing is a safety device that puts an appliance at zero potential. A thick copper wire connects the metal body of an appliance to a copper plate buried deep in the ground.

If the live wire touches the metal body accidentally, the current flows through the earth wire to the ground instead of through the user, preventing electric shock.
content.teacherTip: Earth is like a giant sponge that soaks up extra electricity.
content.examTip: Explain that earthing protects from "electric shocks."

content.question: What is a fuse? State two properties of fuse wire. Where is it connected?
content.answer:
FUSE: A safety device used to limit current in an electric circuit. It melts and breaks the circuit when current exceeds safe limit.

Properties of fuse wire:
(i) High resistance
(ii) Low melting point

Fuse is always connected in the LIVE wire.
content.teacherTip: A fuse "sacrifices itself" to protect appliances.
content.examTip: Both properties are essential for full marks.

content.question: What is short circuiting? What is overloading? How can they be prevented?
content.answer:
SHORT CIRCUITING: When insulation breaks and live wire touches neutral wire directly. Causes sparking and fire.

OVERLOADING: When too many appliances draw more current than the circuit is designed for.

Prevention: Use fuse or MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) in the live wire. Use proper insulation. Don't plug too many appliances in one socket.
content.teacherTip: Short circuit = "accidental shortcut" for electricity.
content.examTip: Define both terms and mention prevention methods.

content.question: State the colour coding of wires. Why are appliances connected in parallel?
content.answer:
Colour Coding:
• Live wire: Red or Brown
• Neutral wire: Black or Light Blue
• Earth wire: Green or Yellow

Appliances are connected in PARALLEL because:
(i) Each appliance gets full 220 V voltage
(ii) If one appliance fails, others continue to work
(iii) Each can be switched on/off independently
content.teacherTip: "Red is for Danger" (Live), "Green is for Earth" (Ground).
content.examTip: Memorize both old and new colour codes.

content.question: Describe the transmission of electric power from generating station to homes.
content.answer:
1. GENERATING STATION: Power generated at 11 kV
2. GRID SUB-STATION: Stepped UP to 132 kV (to minimize energy loss in long-distance transmission)
3. MAIN SUB-STATION: Stepped down to 33 kV
4. CITY SUB-STATION: Stepped down to 220 V for household supply

High voltage reduces current, which reduces heat loss (I²R) in transmission wires.
content.teacherTip: Step up for long distance, step down for safety.
content.examTip: Know the sequence: 11 kV → 132 kV → 33 kV → 220 V.

content.content: Plants need a transport system to carry water and minerals from roots to leaves, and food (sugars) from leaves to all other parts. Unlike animals, plants do not have a heart — they rely on special tissues called XYLEM and PHLOEM.
content.explanation: Roots absorb water from soil, but photosynthesis happens in leaves. A transport system bridges this gap so every cell gets what it needs.
content.teacherTip: Think of xylem as the "water pipe" and phloem as the "food delivery truck."
content.examTip: Always name both xylem AND phloem when asked about plant transport tissues.

content.content: XYLEM carries water and dissolved minerals UPWARD from roots to leaves. It is made of dead, hollow cells forming continuous tubes. The movement is driven by TRANSPIRATION PULL — as water evaporates from leaves, it creates a suction that pulls water up.
content.explanation: Imagine drinking through a straw — when you suck from the top, liquid rises from the bottom. Transpiration creates a similar pull in the plant.
content.teacherTip: Xylem = dead cells, one-way (UP only). Remember "X-UP" — X for xylem, UP for direction.
content.examTip: Mention "transpiration pull" as the driving force for water transport in xylem.

content.content: PHLOEM carries prepared food (mainly sucrose) from leaves to all parts of the plant. This process is called TRANSLOCATION. Phloem is made of living cells called sieve tubes with companion cells. Food moves both UP and DOWN.
content.explanation: After photosynthesis makes sugar in leaves, phloem delivers it everywhere — to roots for storage, to fruits for sweetness, to growing tips for energy.
content.teacherTip: Phloem = living cells, two-way (up AND down). Remember "Phloem = Food."
content.examTip: Use the term "translocation" specifically for food transport in phloem.

content.content: TRANSPIRATION is the loss of water vapour from the aerial parts of a plant, mainly through tiny pores called STOMATA on leaves. It helps in: (i) pulling water upward, (ii) cooling the plant, (iii) helping mineral absorption. Factors affecting transpiration: temperature, humidity, wind, light intensity.
content.explanation: On a hot, dry, windy day, plants lose water faster. Each leaf has thousands of stomata that open and close to regulate water loss.
content.teacherTip: Transpiration is like the plant "sweating" — it cools the plant and drives water flow.
content.examTip: List at least 3 functions of transpiration for full marks.

content.content: Root hairs are thin extensions of root cells that increase the surface area for absorbing water and minerals from soil. Water enters root hairs by OSMOSIS (movement of water from a region of higher water concentration to lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane).
content.explanation: Root hairs are like tiny fingers reaching into the soil to grab as much water as possible. More surface area = more absorption.
content.teacherTip: Root hair cell walls are thin and have no cuticle — perfectly designed for absorption.
content.examTip: Define osmosis precisely — mention "semi-permeable membrane" for full marks.

content.question: Xylem carries food from leaves to other parts of the plant.
content.answer: False. Xylem carries WATER and minerals upward. PHLOEM carries food (translocation).
content.teacherTip: Xylem = Water, Phloem = Food. Never mix them up!
content.examTip: This is one of the most commonly tested mix-ups in biology exams.

content.question: Transpiration helps in the upward movement of water in plants.
content.answer: True. Transpiration creates a suction force (transpiration pull) that draws water upward through the xylem.
content.teacherTip: Transpiration pull is the main driving force for water movement in tall trees.
content.examTip: Connect transpiration to "pull" or "suction" for scientific accuracy.

content.question: Phloem is made up of dead cells.
content.answer: False. Phloem is made of LIVING cells (sieve tubes and companion cells). Xylem is made of dead cells.
content.teacherTip: Living = Phloem, Dead = Xylem.
content.examTip: This distinction between xylem and phloem is a very common exam question.

content.question: Water enters root hair cells by osmosis.
content.answer: True. Soil water has higher water concentration than root hair cell sap, so water moves in by osmosis through the semi-permeable cell membrane.
content.teacherTip: Osmosis is a special type of diffusion — only for water through a membrane.
content.examTip: Always mention "semi-permeable membrane" when defining osmosis.

content.question: The tissue that transports water in plants is called ______.
content.answer: Xylem transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves.
content.teacherTip: X for Xylem, X marks the spot where water goes UP.
content.examTip: Spell "xylem" correctly — common spelling mistake in exams.

content.question: The transport of food in phloem is called ______.
content.answer: Translocation is the movement of food materials (mainly sucrose) through phloem from leaves to other plant parts.
content.teacherTip: "Trans" = across, "location" = places. Food moves across locations!
content.examTip: Use "translocation" (not "transportation") specifically for phloem food movement.

content.question: The tiny pores on leaf surface through which transpiration occurs are called ______.
content.answer: Stomata (singular: stoma) are tiny pores on the leaf surface surrounded by guard cells that control their opening and closing.
content.teacherTip: Guard cells are like bouncers at a club door — they decide when stomata open or close.
content.examTip: Mention "guard cells" when discussing stomata for extra marks.

content.question: Which tissue carries water upward in plants?
content.answer: Xylem carries water and dissolved minerals upward from roots to leaves. It consists of dead, hollow cells.
content.teacherTip: Xylem = Water highway going UP.
content.examTip: If the question mentions "upward" and "water," the answer is always xylem.

content.question: Transpiration mainly occurs through:
content.answer: Most transpiration (about 90%) occurs through stomata on leaves. A small amount occurs through the stem (lenticels).
content.teacherTip: Leaves are the main transpiration organs because they have thousands of stomata.
content.examTip: Specify "stomata on leaves" rather than just "leaves" for precision.

content.question: Differentiate between xylem and phloem.
content.answer:
XYLEM:
• Transports water and minerals
• Direction: upward only (roots → leaves)
• Made of dead, hollow cells
• Driven by transpiration pull

PHLOEM:
• Transports food (sugars)
• Direction: both up and down
• Made of living cells (sieve tubes)
• Process called translocation
content.teacherTip: Make a comparison table with at least 4 points for full marks.
content.examTip: Always compare at least 4 features: what they carry, direction, cell type, and process name.

content.question: What is transpiration? State three of its functions.
content.answer:
TRANSPIRATION: The loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through stomata on leaves.

Functions:
1. Creates transpiration pull that helps in upward movement of water
2. Cools the plant surface (evaporative cooling)
3. Helps in absorption and transport of minerals from soil
4. Maintains water balance in the plant
content.teacherTip: Transpiration = plant sweating. It cools AND pulls water up.
content.examTip: List at least 3 functions clearly numbered for full marks.

content.question: Define osmosis. How does it help in water absorption by roots?
content.answer:
OSMOSIS: The movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.

In roots: Soil water (dilute solution) has higher water concentration than root hair cell sap (concentrated solution). So water moves from soil INTO root hair cells by osmosis through the semi-permeable cell membrane. This absorbed water then passes from cell to cell until it reaches the xylem.
content.teacherTip: Osmosis always goes from dilute to concentrated — water follows the "thirsty" cells.
content.examTip: The three key parts of osmosis definition: water molecules, concentration gradient, semi-permeable membrane.

content.content: Plants reproduce by two methods: (1) ASEXUAL REPRODUCTION — new plant from a single parent without seeds (e.g., budding, fragmentation, vegetative propagation, spore formation). (2) SEXUAL REPRODUCTION — involves fusion of male and female gametes, usually produces seeds.
content.explanation: Asexual = clone of parent (identical). Sexual = mix of two parents (variation). Both have advantages in different situations.
content.teacherTip: Asexual = one parent, identical offspring. Sexual = two parents, varied offspring.
content.examTip: Give examples for each type when asked to differentiate.

content.content: A typical flower has 4 whorls: (1) SEPALS (calyx) — protect the bud. (2) PETALS (corolla) — attract pollinators with colour/scent. (3) STAMENS (androecium) — male part, produces pollen in anthers. (4) PISTIL/CARPEL (gynoecium) — female part, has stigma, style, and ovary containing ovules.
content.explanation: The stamen (male) makes pollen grains. The pistil (female) contains egg cells in ovules. When pollen reaches the stigma, fertilization can begin.
content.teacherTip: Remember: "Stamen = Stamina = Male strength" and "Pistil = Female."
content.examTip: Draw and label a flower diagram — it appears in almost every exam.

content.content: POLLINATION: Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. Self-pollination = same flower. Cross-pollination = different flower (by wind, insects, water, birds). FERTILIZATION: Pollen tube grows down the style, male gamete fuses with egg cell in the ovule. After fertilization: ovule → seed, ovary → fruit.
content.explanation: Pollination is just delivery of pollen. Fertilization is the actual fusion. After fertilization, the ovary swells to become the fruit protecting the seeds inside.
content.teacherTip: Pollination = postal delivery. Fertilization = opening the letter (fusion).
content.examTip: Clearly distinguish pollination from fertilization — they are NOT the same thing.

content.content: Seeds must be scattered away from parent plant to reduce competition. Methods: (1) WIND — light seeds with wings/parachutes (dandelion, maple). (2) WATER — seeds that float (coconut). (3) ANIMALS — hooks/spines that stick to fur, or fleshy fruits eaten by animals. (4) EXPLOSION — pods burst open to fling seeds (pea, balsam).
content.explanation: If all seeds fell directly under the parent tree, they would compete for sunlight, water, and nutrients. Dispersal gives each seed a better chance of survival.
content.teacherTip: Each dispersal method matches the seed's structure — hooks for animals, wings for wind.
content.examTip: Give one example for each method of seed dispersal.

content.question: The__(stamen) is the female reproductive part of a flower.
content.answer: False. The stamen is the MALE part (produces pollen). The PISTIL (carpel) is the female part.
content.teacherTip: Stamen = Male. Pistil = Female. A very common trick question!
content.examTip: If confused, remember: Pistil has the ovary which produces eggs (female).

content.question: After fertilization, the ovary develops into a fruit.
content.answer: True. The ovary wall becomes the fruit wall, and the ovules inside become seeds.
content.teacherTip: Ovary → Fruit. Ovule → Seed. Simple transformation!
content.examTip: State both transformations (ovary→fruit AND ovule→seed) for complete answers.

content.question: Cross-pollination occurs when pollen is transferred within the same flower.
content.answer: False. That is SELF-pollination. CROSS-pollination is transfer of pollen from one flower to another flower (of the same species).
content.teacherTip: Cross = across different flowers. Self = same flower.
content.examTip: Define both self and cross-pollination when asked about either one.

content.question: The male part of a flower is called ______.
content.answer: The stamen consists of the anther (which produces pollen) and the filament (which supports the anther).
content.teacherTip: The anther is like a pollen factory sitting on top of a stalk (filament).
content.examTip: Name both parts of the stamen (anther + filament) for extra marks.

content.question: After fertilization, the ovule develops into a ______.
content.answer: The ovule contains the egg cell. After fertilization (fusion with male gamete), the ovule develops into a seed containing the embryo.
content.teacherTip: Ovule → Seed. The embryo inside the seed is the baby plant!
content.examTip: Mention that the seed contains an embryo (future plant).

content.question: Which part of the flower develops into fruit?
content.answer: The ovary develops into the fruit after fertilization. The ovary wall becomes the fruit wall (pericarp).
content.teacherTip: Ovary = future fruit. Think of ovary as "fruit-in-waiting."
content.examTip: Mention "after fertilization" to show the cause-effect relationship.

content.question: Dandelion seeds are dispersed by:
content.answer: Dandelion seeds have light, feathery parachute-like structures that help them float in the wind over long distances.
content.teacherTip: Dandelions blow in the wind — classic wind dispersal!
content.examTip: Describe the structural adaptation (parachute/wings) that aids wind dispersal.

content.question: Draw a labelled diagram of a flower and name its four main whorls.
content.answer:
The four whorls of a flower (from outside to inside):

1. CALYX (Sepals) — Green, leaf-like, protects the flower bud
2. COROLLA (Petals) — Colourful, attracts pollinators
3. ANDROECIUM (Stamens) — Male part, each stamen has anther + filament
4. GYNOECIUM (Pistil/Carpel) — Female part, has stigma + style + ovary

The ovary contains ovules which become seeds after fertilization.
content.teacherTip: Draw from outside in: Sepals → Petals → Stamens → Pistil (center).
content.examTip: Label at least 6 parts: sepal, petal, anther, filament, stigma, ovary.

content.content: Humans reproduce sexually. MALE system: Testes produce sperm and testosterone. Sperm travel through vas deferens. FEMALE system: Ovaries produce eggs (ova) and hormones (estrogen, progesterone). Eggs travel through fallopian tubes (oviducts) to the uterus. Fertilization occurs in the fallopian tube.
content.explanation: The male produces millions of tiny sperm. The female produces one egg per month. When a sperm meets the egg and fuses with it, fertilization occurs and a new life begins.
content.teacherTip: Testes = sperm factory. Ovaries = egg factory. Fallopian tube = meeting point.
content.examTip: Name the organs AND their functions for complete answers.

content.content: FERTILIZATION: Fusion of sperm and egg to form a ZYGOTE in the fallopian tube. The zygote divides repeatedly to form an EMBRYO, which implants in the uterus wall. The PLACENTA provides oxygen and nutrients from mother's blood to the embryo (now called FOETUS). After about 9 months (gestation period), the baby is born.
content.explanation: The placenta acts as a bridge between mother and baby — exchanging nutrients, oxygen, and waste WITHOUT mixing their blood directly.
content.teacherTip: Zygote → Embryo → Foetus → Baby. Each stage has a specific name.
content.examTip: Define: zygote, embryo, foetus, placenta, and gestation period.

content.question: Fertilization in humans occurs in the uterus.
content.answer: False. Fertilization occurs in the FALLOPIAN TUBE (oviduct). The fertilized egg (zygote) then travels to the uterus for implantation.
content.teacherTip: Fallopian tube = fertilization. Uterus = implantation and growth.
content.examTip: This is a very common trick question — fertilization is NOT in the uterus.

content.question: The placenta provides nutrients to the developing embryo.
content.answer: True. The placenta is a disc-shaped organ that connects the foetus to the mother's uterine wall. It transfers oxygen and nutrients from mother to foetus, and removes waste from foetus to mother.
content.teacherTip: Placenta = lifeline between mother and baby.
content.examTip: Mention that mother's blood and baby's blood do NOT mix directly.

content.question: The fusion of sperm and egg is called:
content.answer: Fertilization is the fusion of the male gamete (sperm) with the female gamete (egg/ovum) to form a zygote.
content.teacherTip: Fertilization = fusion. Pollination is only in plants!
content.examTip: Never use "pollination" for humans — that's only for flowers.

content.question: What is the role of the placenta during pregnancy?
content.answer:
The PLACENTA is a temporary organ that connects the developing foetus to the mother's uterine wall. Its functions:

1. Supplies oxygen and nutrients from mother's blood to the foetus
2. Removes carbon dioxide and waste products from the foetus to the mother
3. Acts as a barrier — prevents mixing of mother's and baby's blood
4. Produces hormones to maintain pregnancy

The foetus is connected to the placenta through the UMBILICAL CORD.
content.teacherTip: Placenta = exchange centre + protective barrier + hormone factory.
content.examTip: Mention the umbilical cord as the physical connection between foetus and placenta.

content.content: An ECOSYSTEM is a community of living organisms (biotic) interacting with their non-living environment (abiotic). Biotic factors: plants, animals, microorganisms. Abiotic factors: sunlight, water, temperature, soil, air. Types: forest, grassland, desert, aquatic (freshwater and marine).
content.explanation: An ecosystem is like a neighbourhood where everyone (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria) depends on each other and shares resources like water, light, and space.
content.teacherTip: Biotic = Bio = Life. Abiotic = A (not) + biotic = non-living.
content.examTip: Always give examples of both biotic AND abiotic factors when defining ecosystem.

content.content: A FOOD CHAIN shows the flow of energy from one organism to another. Starts with PRODUCERS (green plants) → PRIMARY CONSUMERS (herbivores) → SECONDARY CONSUMERS (carnivores) → TERTIARY CONSUMERS (top predators). A FOOD WEB is many interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Energy decreases at each level (10% rule).
content.explanation: Plants capture sun's energy. Herbivores eat plants. Carnivores eat herbivores. At each step, about 90% of energy is lost as heat, so only 10% passes to the next level.
content.teacherTip: Food chain = single line. Food web = tangled network. Web is more realistic.
content.examTip: Always start a food chain with a PRODUCER (green plant). Use arrows pointing in the direction energy flows.

content.content: DECOMPOSERS (bacteria, fungi) break down dead organisms and waste into simple nutrients that return to the soil. This is essential for NUTRIENT CYCLING — carbon, nitrogen, and other elements are recycled between living and non-living parts of the ecosystem. Without decomposers, dead matter would pile up and nutrients would be locked away.
content.explanation: Decomposers are nature's recyclers. They close the loop in an ecosystem by returning nutrients to the soil so plants can use them again.
content.teacherTip: Decomposers = cleanup crew. Without them, Earth would be covered in dead things!
content.examTip: Name bacteria and fungi as the two main types of decomposers.

content.question: A food chain always begins with a producer.
content.answer: True. Producers (green plants) are always the first link in a food chain because they convert solar energy into food through photosynthesis.
content.teacherTip: No producers = no food chain. Plants are the foundation of every ecosystem.
content.examTip: Draw arrows FROM the producer TOWARD consumers (direction of energy flow).

content.question: Energy increases as we move up a food chain.
content.answer: False. Energy DECREASES at each trophic level. Only about 10% of energy passes from one level to the next. The rest is lost as heat.
content.teacherTip: The 10% rule — each level gets only 10% of the previous level's energy.
content.examTip: Explain the 10% rule when discussing energy flow in food chains.

content.question: Which of the following is a decomposer?
content.answer: Mushrooms (fungi) are decomposers. They break down dead organic matter into simpler substances, recycling nutrients back into the soil.
content.teacherTip: Fungi and bacteria = decomposers. They feed on dead and decaying matter.
content.examTip: Common decomposers to remember: bacteria, fungi (mushrooms), earthworms.

content.question: What is a food web? How is it different from a food chain?
content.answer:
FOOD CHAIN: A linear sequence showing the transfer of energy from one organism to the next.
Example: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle

FOOD WEB: A complex network of many interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. It shows that most organisms eat more than one type of food and are eaten by more than one predator.

Difference: A food chain is a single pathway; a food web shows ALL the feeding relationships. Food webs are more realistic because organisms rarely depend on just one food source.
content.teacherTip: A food web is like a "food internet" — everything is connected to everything else.
content.examTip: Always give a specific food chain example when asked about food chains or webs.

content.content: The ENDOCRINE SYSTEM consists of ductless glands that secrete HORMONES directly into the blood. Major glands: PITUITARY (master gland — controls other glands), THYROID (thyroxine — controls metabolism), PANCREAS (insulin — controls blood sugar), ADRENAL (adrenaline — fight-or-flight response). Hormones are chemical messengers that regulate body functions.
content.explanation: Unlike sweat glands that have ducts (tubes), endocrine glands release hormones directly into the bloodstream. Blood carries hormones to target organs throughout the body.
content.teacherTip: Endocrine = Ductless = directly into blood. Exocrine = has ducts (like sweat, saliva).
content.examTip: Know at least 4 glands with their hormones AND functions.

content.content: ADOLESCENCE is the period between childhood and adulthood (roughly ages 11-19). PUBERTY is the onset of sexual maturity when the body changes under the influence of hormones. Changes in boys: facial hair, deeper voice, muscle growth (testosterone). Changes in girls: breast development, menstruation begins, hip widening (estrogen). Common changes: growth spurt, acne, mood changes.
content.explanation: The pituitary gland signals the testes (boys) and ovaries (girls) to start producing sex hormones, which trigger all the physical changes of puberty.
content.teacherTip: Pituitary gland starts puberty by sending signals to reproductive organs.
content.examTip: List changes separately for boys and girls, plus common changes shared by both.

content.question: The pituitary gland is called the master gland.
content.answer: True. The pituitary gland is called the master gland because it controls the functioning of most other endocrine glands by releasing stimulating hormones.
content.teacherTip: Pituitary = boss gland. It tells other glands what to do.
content.examTip: Explain WHY it's called "master gland" — because it controls other glands.

content.question: Insulin is produced by the thyroid gland.
content.answer: False. Insulin is produced by the PANCREAS. The thyroid gland produces thyroxine, which controls metabolism.
content.teacherTip: Pancreas → Insulin → Blood sugar. Thyroid → Thyroxine → Metabolism.
content.examTip: Know which hormone comes from which gland — very commonly tested.

content.question: Which hormone controls blood sugar levels?
content.answer: Insulin (from the pancreas) lowers blood sugar by allowing cells to take in glucose. Deficiency causes diabetes.
content.teacherTip: Insulin = "key" that opens cell doors for sugar to enter.
content.examTip: Mention "diabetes" as the disease caused by insulin deficiency.

content.question: Name four major endocrine glands and their hormones with one function each.
content.answer:
1. PITUITARY GLAND → Growth Hormone → Controls body growth
2. THYROID GLAND → Thyroxine → Controls rate of metabolism
3. PANCREAS → Insulin → Lowers blood sugar level
4. ADRENAL GLAND → Adrenaline → Prepares body for emergency (fight-or-flight)
5. TESTES (male) → Testosterone → Development of male features
6. OVARIES (female) → Estrogen → Development of female features
content.teacherTip: Make a table: Gland | Hormone | Function — easy to remember.
content.examTip: Learn at least 5 gland-hormone-function combos for the exam.

content.content: The HEART is a muscular organ with 4 chambers: 2 atria (upper, receive blood) and 2 ventricles (lower, pump blood out). RIGHT side pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs (pulmonary circulation). LEFT side pumps oxygenated blood to body (systemic circulation). This is called DOUBLE CIRCULATION — blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit.
content.explanation: The heart is actually two pumps side by side. The right pump sends blood to lungs to get oxygen. The left pump sends oxygen-rich blood to the entire body.
content.teacherTip: Right = Lungs (to collect O₂). Left = Body (to deliver O₂). Remember: Right-Lung, Left-Body.
content.examTip: Use the term "double circulation" and explain both pulmonary and systemic circuits.

content.content: BLOOD has 4 components: (1) RED BLOOD CELLS (RBCs) — contain haemoglobin, carry oxygen. (2) WHITE BLOOD CELLS (WBCs) — fight infections (immunity). (3) PLATELETS — help in blood clotting at wound sites. (4) PLASMA — liquid part (55% of blood), carries dissolved nutrients, hormones, waste, and proteins.
content.explanation: Blood is like a delivery service: RBCs deliver oxygen, plasma delivers food and hormones, WBCs are the security team, and platelets are the repair crew.
content.teacherTip: RBC = oxygen taxi. WBC = soldiers. Platelets = band-aids. Plasma = river everything floats in.
content.examTip: Name all 4 components with their functions for full marks.

content.question: Arteries carry oxygenated blood away from the heart.
content.answer: True (with one exception). Arteries carry oxygenated blood FROM heart TO body. Exception: Pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from heart to lungs.
content.teacherTip: Arteries = Away from heart. Veins = toward heart (Veins = in).
content.examTip: ALWAYS mention the exception (pulmonary artery) to show thorough knowledge.

content.question: White blood cells carry oxygen in the blood.
content.answer: False. RED blood cells (RBCs) carry oxygen using haemoglobin. WHITE blood cells (WBCs) fight infections and provide immunity.
content.teacherTip: Red = oxygen. White = defence. Don't mix them up!
content.examTip: Mention "haemoglobin" when talking about oxygen transport by RBCs.

content.question: How many chambers does the human heart have?
content.answer: The human heart has 4 chambers: 2 atria (upper) and 2 ventricles (lower). The septum divides the heart into left and right halves.
content.teacherTip: 4 chambers = 2 atria + 2 ventricles. Septum keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separate.
content.examTip: Name all 4 chambers: left atrium, right atrium, left ventricle, right ventricle.

content.question: What is double circulation? Explain with a diagram description.
content.answer:
DOUBLE CIRCULATION means blood passes through the heart TWICE in one complete cycle.

1. PULMONARY CIRCULATION (Right side):
Right ventricle → Pulmonary artery → Lungs (picks up O₂, drops CO₂) → Pulmonary vein → Left atrium

2. SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION (Left side):
Left ventricle → Aorta → Body organs (delivers O₂, picks up CO₂) → Vena cava → Right atrium

Advantage: Oxygenated blood reaches body at high pressure, ensuring efficient delivery of oxygen to all tissues.
content.teacherTip: Right side = lung circuit. Left side = body circuit. Blood visits heart twice!
content.examTip: Draw the flow as: Body → Right heart → Lungs → Left heart → Body.

content.content: The NERVOUS SYSTEM controls and coordinates all body activities. It has 3 parts: (1) CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS) = Brain + Spinal cord. (2) PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (PNS) = Nerves connecting CNS to all body parts. (3) AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM = Controls involuntary actions (heartbeat, digestion). The basic unit is the NEURON (nerve cell).
content.explanation: The brain is like the CEO, the spinal cord is the highway, and nerves are roads connecting every part of the body to headquarters.
content.teacherTip: CNS = command centre. PNS = communication wires. Autonomic = autopilot.
content.examTip: Name all 3 divisions of the nervous system for a complete answer.

content.content: The brain has 3 main parts: (1) CEREBRUM — largest part, controls thinking, memory, reasoning, voluntary actions, and senses. (2) CEREBELLUM — behind cerebrum, controls balance, posture, and coordination of movements. (3) MEDULLA OBLONGATA — base of brain, controls involuntary actions like breathing, heartbeat, swallowing, and blood pressure.
content.explanation: Cerebrum = thinking brain (biggest). Cerebellum = balance brain (behind). Medulla = survival brain (bottom) — controls things you don't think about.
content.teacherTip: "Cerebrum = Cerebral = Thinking. Cerebellum = Balance (like a ballerina). Medulla = Must-do (breathing, heartbeat)."
content.examTip: Know at least 2 functions for each part of the brain.

content.content: A REFLEX ACTION is an automatic, rapid, involuntary response to a stimulus. Example: pulling hand away from a hot object. The pathway is called a REFLEX ARC: Receptor → Sensory nerve → Spinal cord → Motor nerve → Effector (muscle/gland). Reflexes are controlled by the SPINAL CORD, not the brain — this makes them faster.
content.explanation: When you touch something hot, you pull your hand away BEFORE you even feel the pain. The spinal cord handles the response immediately without waiting for the brain.
content.teacherTip: Reflex = shortcut through spinal cord. Brain finds out AFTER the action!
content.examTip: Draw the reflex arc and label all 5 components for full marks.

content.question: Reflex actions are controlled by the brain.
content.answer: False. Reflex actions are controlled by the SPINAL CORD. This makes them faster because the signal doesn't need to travel all the way to the brain.
content.teacherTip: Reflexes bypass the brain — spinal cord handles them directly.
content.examTip: Mention "speed" as the advantage of spinal cord control.

content.question: The cerebellum controls balance and coordination.
content.answer: True. The cerebellum maintains body balance, posture, and coordinates precise voluntary movements like writing, typing, or catching a ball.
content.teacherTip: Cerebellum = "little brain" at the back = balance + coordination.
content.examTip: Give examples of actions controlled by cerebellum (walking, cycling, catching).

content.question: Which part of the brain controls involuntary actions like breathing?
content.answer: The medulla oblongata controls vital involuntary functions: breathing, heartbeat, blood pressure, swallowing, coughing, and sneezing.
content.teacherTip: Medulla = "autopilot" for survival. Damage here is life-threatening.
content.examTip: List at least 3 involuntary actions controlled by the medulla.

content.question: What is a reflex action? Describe the reflex arc with an example.
content.answer:
REFLEX ACTION: An automatic, quick, involuntary response to a stimulus, controlled by the spinal cord.

Example: Touching a hot object and pulling hand away.

REFLEX ARC pathway:
1. RECEPTOR (skin) — detects the hot stimulus
2. SENSORY NERVE — carries impulse from receptor to spinal cord
3. SPINAL CORD — processes the information and generates response
4. MOTOR NERVE — carries impulse from spinal cord to effector
5. EFFECTOR (muscle) — contracts and pulls the hand away

Advantage: Protects the body from harm by responding faster than conscious thought.
content.teacherTip: 5 steps: Receptor → Sensory → Spinal cord → Motor → Effector. Learn the order!
content.examTip: Drawing the reflex arc as a diagram scores higher than just writing text.

content.content: Diseases are classified as: (1) COMMUNICABLE (infectious) — caused by microorganisms, can spread person to person. Examples: malaria (protozoa), tuberculosis (bacteria), common cold (virus), ringworm (fungi). (2) NON-COMMUNICABLE — cannot spread, caused by lifestyle/genetics. Examples: diabetes, cancer, heart disease, allergies.
content.explanation: Communicable diseases have a pathogen (germ) that can travel from one person to another through air, water, food, or contact. Non-communicable diseases stay with the person.
content.teacherTip: Communicable = Catching = Contagious. Non-communicable = Not catching.
content.examTip: Give the causative organism for each communicable disease you mention.

content.content: Communicable diseases spread through: (1) AIR — coughing/sneezing (cold, TB, flu). (2) WATER — contaminated drinking water (cholera, typhoid). (3) FOOD — unhygienic food (food poisoning, dysentery). (4) CONTACT — direct touching or body fluids (ringworm, AIDS). (5) VECTORS — organisms that carry disease (mosquitoes carry malaria and dengue, houseflies carry cholera).
content.explanation: A vector is an organism that doesn't cause the disease itself but carries the pathogen from an infected person to a healthy person. Mosquitoes are the most dangerous vectors.
content.teacherTip: A vector is a "disease taxi" — it gives the germ a ride to its next victim.
content.examTip: Name the vector AND the disease it carries (e.g., female Anopheles mosquito → malaria).

content.content: FIRST AID is the immediate help given to an injured or sick person before medical help arrives. Key principles: (1) BURNS — cool with running water for 10+ minutes. (2) BLEEDING — apply firm pressure with clean cloth, raise the wound. (3) FRACTURES — immobilize the area, don't try to set the bone. (4) INSECT STINGS — remove stinger, apply cold compress. (5) FAINTING — lay person flat, raise legs, ensure fresh air.
content.explanation: First aid is about stabilizing the person, not curing them. The goal is to prevent the condition from getting worse until a doctor takes over.
content.teacherTip: Remember: DON'T panic, DON'T move fractures, DON'T apply ice directly on burns.
content.examTip: For each injury, state what TO do and what NOT to do.

content.question: Malaria is caused by a virus.
content.answer: False. Malaria is caused by a PROTOZOAN parasite called Plasmodium. It is transmitted by the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito.
content.teacherTip: Malaria = Plasmodium (protozoa) via Anopheles mosquito. Not a virus!
content.examTip: Name both the causative organism AND the vector for malaria.

content.question: Non-communicable diseases can spread from person to person.
content.answer: False. Non-communicable diseases (like diabetes, cancer, heart disease) CANNOT spread from person to person. They are caused by genetics, lifestyle, or environmental factors.
content.teacherTip: Non-communicable = Non-contagious. They stay with the individual.
content.examTip: Give 2-3 examples of non-communicable diseases in your answer.

content.question: Which organism transmits malaria?
content.answer: The FEMALE Anopheles mosquito transmits malaria. Only females bite (they need blood for egg development). The male Anopheles feeds on plant nectar.
content.teacherTip: Only FEMALE Anopheles bites. Male mosquitoes are vegetarians!
content.examTip: Specify "female" Anopheles — writing just "Anopheles mosquito" may lose marks.

content.question: Differentiate between communicable and non-communicable diseases. Give two examples of each.
content.answer:
COMMUNICABLE DISEASES:
• Caused by pathogens (bacteria, virus, fungi, protozoa)
• CAN spread from person to person
• Spread through air, water, food, contact, or vectors
• Examples: Malaria (protozoa), Tuberculosis (bacteria), Common cold (virus)

NON-COMMUNICABLE DISEASES:
• NOT caused by pathogens
• CANNOT spread from person to person
• Caused by genetics, lifestyle, diet, or environmental factors
• Examples: Diabetes, Cancer, Heart disease, Allergies
content.teacherTip: Communicable = germ + spread. Non-communicable = no germ + no spread.
content.examTip: Compare at least 4 features and give 2+ examples for each type.

content.content: AGRICULTURE is the science of cultivating land, growing crops, and raising livestock. Steps in crop production: (1) PREPARATION OF SOIL — ploughing, levelling. (2) SOWING — planting seeds at correct depth and spacing. (3) ADDING MANURE/FERTILIZERS — for nutrients. (4) IRRIGATION — supplying water. (5) WEEDING — removing unwanted plants. (6) HARVESTING — collecting mature crops. (7) STORAGE — protecting from moisture, pests.
content.explanation: Farming is a step-by-step process. Each step matters — wrong seed depth, too little water, or pest damage can ruin an entire harvest.
content.teacherTip: Remember the order: Prepare → Sow → Nourish → Water → Weed → Harvest → Store.
content.examTip: List all 7 steps in order when asked about crop production practices.

content.content: MANURE: Natural, organic material made from decomposed plant/animal waste (compost, farmyard manure). Improves soil structure, adds humus, slow-release nutrients. FERTILIZERS: Chemical/synthetic substances (NPK — Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium). Quick-release, specific nutrient supply, but can harm soil health if overused.
content.explanation: Manure is like a balanced home-cooked meal for the soil. Fertilizers are like supplements — fast-acting but can cause problems if used too much (soil pollution, water contamination).
content.teacherTip: Manure = natural + slow + improves soil. Fertilizer = chemical + fast + can damage soil.
content.examTip: Compare at least 4 differences between manure and fertilizers.

content.content: ANIMAL HUSBANDRY is the scientific management of livestock for food and other products. Includes: DAIRY FARMING (milk, cheese, butter), POULTRY FARMING (eggs, chicken meat), FISH FARMING (aquaculture), BEE KEEPING (apiculture — honey, beeswax). Proper shelter, balanced feed, disease prevention, and breeding are essential.
content.explanation: Just like crops need care, farm animals need proper food, clean shelter, vaccinations, and good breeding to produce maximum quality products.
content.teacherTip: Apiculture = bees (Api = Latin for bee). Aquaculture = fish. Poultry = birds.
content.examTip: Know the specific terms: apiculture, aquaculture, poultry, dairy farming.

content.question: Fertilizers are always better than manure for crops.
content.answer: False. While fertilizers provide quick nutrients, excessive use can damage soil structure, pollute water bodies, and reduce soil fertility long-term. Manure improves soil texture and adds humus.
content.teacherTip: Fertilizers = fast but risky. Manure = slow but sustainable.
content.examTip: Mention disadvantages of excessive fertilizer use: soil degradation, water pollution.

content.question: Apiculture refers to the rearing of fish.
content.answer: False. Apiculture is BEE-KEEPING (for honey and beeswax). Fish rearing is called AQUACULTURE or pisciculture.
content.teacherTip: Api = bee (Latin). Aqua = water. Pisci = fish. Learn the Latin roots!
content.examTip: Know: Apiculture=bees, Aquaculture=fish, Sericulture=silkworms, Poultry=birds.

content.question: NPK in fertilizers stands for:
content.answer: NPK stands for Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K — from Kalium). These are the three primary nutrients needed for plant growth.
content.teacherTip: N = leaf growth. P = root/flower growth. K = overall plant health.
content.examTip: Remember K = Potassium (from Latin "Kalium"), not just "K."

content.question: Differentiate between manure and fertilizers.
content.answer:
MANURE:
• Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste)
• Provides nutrients slowly over time
• Improves soil structure and water-holding capacity
• Adds humus to soil
• Eco-friendly, no pollution
• Example: Compost, farmyard manure, vermicompost

FERTILIZERS:
• Chemical/synthetic substances manufactured in factories
• Provides nutrients quickly in specific amounts
• Does NOT improve soil structure
• No humus added
• Overuse causes soil and water pollution
• Example: Urea, NPK, superphosphate
content.teacherTip: Manure = slow food for soil. Fertilizer = fast food for soil (convenient but not always healthy).
content.examTip: Compare at least 5 features in a table format for maximum marks.

content.content: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. It exists in three states: SOLID (fixed shape and volume, particles tightly packed), LIQUID (fixed volume but no fixed shape, particles loosely arranged), GAS (no fixed shape or volume, particles far apart and move freely).
content.explanation: Everything around you — your desk, water, air — is matter. The state depends on how strongly particles attract each other and how much energy they have.
content.teacherTip: Solid = ice cubes stacked tight. Liquid = water flowing. Gas = steam escaping.
content.examTip: Always describe particles arrangement AND intermolecular forces for each state.

content.content: States of matter can change: Melting (solid→liquid at melting point), Boiling (liquid→gas at boiling point), Condensation (gas→liquid on cooling), Freezing (liquid→solid on cooling), Sublimation (solid→gas directly), Deposition (gas→solid). Temperature remains constant during change of state.
content.explanation: Adding heat gives particles more energy to break free. Removing heat slows them down so they lock together again.
content.teacherTip: Draw the change-of-state cycle as a triangle: Solid ↔ Liquid ↔ Gas, with Sublimation as a shortcut.
content.examTip: State that temperature remains CONSTANT during change of state — this is about latent heat.

content.content: DIFFUSION is the spontaneous mixing of particles of two substances due to their random motion. It occurs in gases (fastest), liquids (slower), and very slowly in solids. Example: smell of perfume spreads across a room.
content.explanation: Particles are always moving randomly. When two substances come in contact, their particles intermingle without any stirring needed.
content.teacherTip: Diffusion is fastest in gases because gas particles move the fastest and have the most space.
content.examTip: Rate of diffusion: Gases > Liquids > Solids. Mention this order for full marks.

content.question: Gases have a definite shape and volume.
content.answer: False. Gases have NEITHER definite shape NOR definite volume. They expand to fill any container completely.
content.teacherTip: Only solids have both definite shape and volume.
content.examTip: Solids = both definite. Liquids = definite volume only. Gases = neither.

content.question: Diffusion is fastest in gases.
content.answer: True. Gas particles have the most kinetic energy and the largest intermolecular spaces, allowing them to mix the fastest.
content.teacherTip: More space + more speed = faster mixing.
content.examTip: Compare diffusion rates across all three states when answering.

content.question: The change of a solid directly into gas is called ______.
content.answer: Sublimation. Examples: camphor, dry ice, naphthalene balls.
content.teacherTip: Sub-limation = solid "skips" the liquid step.
content.examTip: Always give an example substance when mentioning sublimation.

content.question: Which state of matter has maximum intermolecular space?
content.answer: Gas. Particles in a gas are far apart with negligible attractive forces, which is why gases can be compressed easily.
content.teacherTip: More space = easier to compress. Gases are highly compressible.
content.examTip: Link intermolecular space to compressibility in your answers.

content.question: Compare solids, liquids, and gases based on shape, volume, compressibility, and particle arrangement.
content.answer:
SOLIDS: Definite shape, definite volume, incompressible, particles tightly packed vibrating in fixed positions.

LIQUIDS: No definite shape (takes container shape), definite volume, slightly compressible, particles close but can slide past each other.

GASES: No definite shape or volume, highly compressible, particles far apart moving randomly at high speed.
content.teacherTip: Make a 4-column table: Property | Solid | Liquid | Gas.
content.examTip: Compare at least 4 properties for maximum marks.

content.content:
PHYSICAL CHANGE: Change in physical properties (shape, size, state) without forming new substance. Reversible. No change in chemical composition. Examples: melting ice, dissolving sugar, breaking glass.
CHEMICAL CHANGE: New substance formed with different properties. Usually irreversible. Involves energy change. Examples: rusting iron, burning wood, cooking food.
content.explanation: Tearing paper is physical (still paper). Burning paper is chemical (becomes ash + CO₂ — completely new substances).
content.teacherTip: Physical = same substance, different look. Chemical = different substance entirely.
content.examTip: Give at least 4 differences with examples of each type.

content.content: How to identify a chemical change: (1) Change in colour (iron turning brown/rusty), (2) Evolution of gas (bubbles when vinegar meets baking soda), (3) Change in temperature (exothermic/endothermic), (4) Formation of precipitate (insoluble solid), (5) Change in smell (milk turning sour).
content.explanation: If you see bubbling, colour change, heat release, or a new smell — a chemical reaction is probably happening.
content.teacherTip: Remember: "Colour, Gas, Heat, Precipitate, Smell" — the 5 signs.
content.examTip: List at least 3 signs with examples when identifying a chemical change.

content.question: Melting of ice is a chemical change.
content.answer: False. Melting of ice is a PHYSICAL change. Water changes state (solid→liquid) but its chemical composition (H₂O) remains the same. It is also reversible.
content.teacherTip: Change of state = physical change. Always.
